title: AI Agents
body:
Overview

Every FarmClaw workspace comes with at least one AI agent — your farm's assistant. Out of the box, a single general-purpose agent handles grain bids, weather, scale tickets, and anything else you ask about.

But FarmClaw also supports multiple agents per workspace. Each agent can have its own personality, its own skill set, and its own Discord channel assignment. Think of it as building a small team of specialists for your operation.

Single agent vs. multi-agent

Single agent (the default)

When you first set up FarmClaw, you get one agent with the General Advisor personality. It has access to all your enabled skills and handles every channel in your Discord server plus DMs.

This is the simplest setup and works well for most operations. One agent that does everything.

Multi-agent setup

As your operation grows or you want more focused responses, you can add specialist agents:

- A Grain Specialist that lives in grain-desk and knows cash bids inside and out
- A Weather agent in weather that handles forecasts, spray windows, and GDD tracking
- A Scale Ticket Clerk in scale-tickets that logs deliveries quickly and precisely
- A Field Operations manager for equipment scheduling and input tracking

Each specialist is tuned for its area. The Grain Specialist leads with numbers and basis comparisons. The Weather agent is cautious and safety-first. The Ticket Clerk is fast and precise. They each know their boundaries and will delegate to other agents when a question falls outside their expertise.

You can always add more agents later. Start with the default General Advisor and see how it handles your day-to-day questions. When you find yourself wanting more focused responses on a specific topic, add a specialist.

What makes up an agent

Each agent in your FarmClaw workspace has these configurable properties:

| Property | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| Display name | The name shown in Discord (e.g., "Farm Assistant", "Grain Desk") |
| Personality preset | Determines the agent's tone, focus area, and boundaries |
| Skills | Which FarmClaw skills this agent can use (grain bids, weather, scale tickets) |
| Channel binding | Which Discord channel this agent is assigned to (optional) |
| Cron jobs | Scheduled messages like morning weather or daily grain bids |
| Default status | Whether this is the default agent (handles unbound channels and DMs) |

Skills

FarmClaw currently offers three core skills that can be assigned to agents:

| Skill | What it does |
|-------|-------------|
| Grain Bids | Scrapes configured elevator websites for current cash grain prices |
| Weather | NWS forecasts, spray window analysis, and GDD calculations |
| Scale Tickets | Log grain deliveries via photo OCR, voice dictation, or guided entry |

Each agent can have any combination of skills. A Grain Specialist typically only has the Grain Bids skill. A General Advisor might have all three.

Cron jobs

Agents can run scheduled tasks that post messages to their assigned channel automatically:

| Cron Job | Schedule | Required Skill |
|----------|----------|---------------|
| Morning Weather | 6:00 AM daily | Weather |
| Daily Grain Bids | 9:00 AM Mon-Fri | Grain Bids |

Cron jobs are optional. Enable them per agent based on what information you want delivered proactively.

Inter-agent communication

When you have multiple agents, they can talk to each other. If someone asks the Farm Assistant a detailed grain marketing question, it can delegate to the Grain Specialist using built-in communication tools (agentslist and sessionssend).

The farmer sees a seamless experience — they ask a question in any channel, and the right agent handles it.

Next steps

- Multi-Agent Setups — Pre-built 2, 3, and 4-agent configurations you can deploy with one click
- Personality Presets — Detailed breakdown of each personality type and its boundaries
- Channel Configuration — How to bind agents to specific Discord channels

title: Multi-Agent Setups
body:
Overview

FarmClaw offers three pre-built multi-agent configurations that you can deploy with one click. Each setup is designed around real farm workflows — you pick the configuration that matches your operation's needs, and FarmClaw handles the rest.

You can also build a custom setup from scratch, but these suggested configurations are a good starting point for most operations.

Suggested setups

Two-Agent: General + Grain

An all-purpose assistant for daily operations and a specialist for grain markets and elevator bids.

| Agent | Personality | Skills | Cron Jobs | Default |
|-------|------------|--------|-----------|---------|
| Farm Assistant | General Advisor | Grain Bids, Weather, Scale Tickets | Morning Weather (6:00 AM) | Yes |
| Grain Desk | Grain Specialist | Grain Bids | Daily Grain Bids (9:00 AM Mon-Fri) | No |

Best for: Operations that want a dedicated grain market channel with daily bid updates, while keeping one general agent for everything else.

Typical channel setup:

| Channel | Agent |
|---------|-------|
| grain-desk | Grain Desk |
| All other channels + DMs | Farm Assistant |

The two-agent configuration is the most common choice. It gives you a focused grain channel without overcomplicating things.

---

Three-Agent: General + Grain + Scale Tickets

Full coverage: general operations, grain marketing, and a dedicated clerk for logging deliveries.

| Agent | Personality | Skills | Cron Jobs | Default |
|-------|------------|--------|-----------|---------|
| Farm Assistant | General Advisor | Grain Bids, Weather | Morning Weather (6:00 AM) | Yes |
| Grain Desk | Grain Specialist | Grain Bids | Daily Grain Bids (9:00 AM Mon-Fri) | No |
| Ticket Clerk | Scale Ticket Clerk | Scale Tickets | None | No |

Best for: Operations that haul a lot of grain and want a fast, focused ticket logging experience separate from general conversation.

Typical channel setup:

| Channel | Agent |
|---------|-------|
| grain-desk | Grain Desk |
| scale-tickets | Ticket Clerk |
| All other channels + DMs | Farm Assistant |

The Ticket Clerk is optimized for speed. It supports three entry methods — snap a photo of your scale ticket, dictate the numbers, or walk through a guided form. Having it in its own channel keeps ticket logging clean and separate from other conversations.

---

Four-Agent: Full Team

Four specialists: general advisor, grain markets, weather and field ops, and scale ticket logging.

| Agent | Personality | Skills | Cron Jobs | Default |
|-------|------------|--------|-----------|---------|
| Farm Assistant | General Advisor | Grain Bids, Weather, Scale Tickets | None | Yes |
| Grain Desk | Grain Specialist | Grain Bids | Daily Grain Bids (9:00 AM Mon-Fri) | No |
| Weather Watch | Weather & Field Ops | Weather | Morning Weather (6:00 AM) | No |
| Ticket Clerk | Scale Ticket Clerk | Scale Tickets | None | No |

Best for: Larger operations that want fully specialized channels with dedicated agents and automated daily updates in the right place.

Typical channel setup:

| Channel | Agent |
|---------|-------|
| grain-desk | Grain Desk |
| weather | Weather Watch |
| scale-tickets | Ticket Clerk |
| All other channels + DMs | Farm Assistant |

Notice that in the four-agent setup, the morning weather cron job moves to Weather Watch and is removed from the Farm Assistant. Daily grain bids are posted by Grain Desk. Each cron job runs in the agent's bound channel, so the information lands where it belongs.

How inter-agent delegation works

When you run multiple agents, they can communicate with each other. This is built into each agent's personality — you don't need to configure any delegation rules.

The tools

Each agent has access to two inter-agent communication tools:

| Tool | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| agentslist | Lists all other agents in the workspace, their specialties, and their channel bindings |
| sessionssend | Sends a message or question to another agent and receives a response |

How it works in practice

1. A farmer asks the Farm Assistant in general: "What's the best corn bid right now?"
2. The Farm Assistant recognizes this is a grain marketing question
3. It checks agentslist and sees Grain Desk is available
4. It tells the farmer: "Let me check with our grain specialist..."
5. It uses sessionssend to pass the question to Grain Desk
6. Grain Desk processes the request, pulls elevator bids, and returns the answer
7. The Farm Assistant relays the response in general

The farmer gets a detailed, specialist-quality answer without leaving their channel.

Delegation by preset

Each personality preset includes guidance on when to defer to other agents:

| Agent Preset | Defers grain questions to | Defers weather questions to | Defers ticket questions to | Defers field ops questions to |
|-------------|--------------------------|---------------------------|---------------------------|------------------------------|
| General Advisor | Grain Specialist | Weather & Field Ops | Scale Ticket Clerk | Field Operations |
| Grain Specialist | -- | Weather & Field Ops | Scale Ticket Clerk | Field Operations |
| Weather & Field Ops | Grain Specialist | -- | Scale Ticket Clerk | Field Operations |
| Scale Ticket Clerk | Grain Specialist | Weather & Field Ops | -- | Field Operations |
| Field Operations | Grain Specialist | Weather & Field Ops | Scale Ticket Clerk | -- |

If a specialist agent is not present in the workspace, the delegating agent will handle the question itself with whatever skills it has available. Delegation only happens when the target agent actually exists.

Deploying a suggested setup

1. Go to Settings > Agents in your FarmClaw dashboard
2. Click Suggested Setups
3. Select one of the three configurations
4. Review the agents, skills, and cron jobs
5. Click Deploy

FarmClaw will create all the agents, assign skills and cron jobs, and apply the configuration. Changes take effect on the next message.

Choosing a suggested setup replaces your current agent configuration. If you have custom agents with specific settings, make note of them before deploying a preset. You can always reconfigure agents individually afterward.

Building a custom setup

If the suggested configurations don't match your needs, you can build your own:

1. Go to Settings > Agents
2. Click Add Agent
3. Choose a personality preset (or select Custom to write your own)
4. Assign skills and cron jobs
5. Optionally bind the agent to a Discord channel
6. Repeat for as many agents as you need
7. Make sure one agent is marked as the default

There's no hard limit on the number of agents, but 2-4 agents is the practical sweet spot. More agents mean more specialized responses but also more complexity in routing and delegation.

Next steps

- Personality Presets — Detailed breakdown of what each preset does and its boundaries
- Channel Configuration — How to bind agents to Discord channels
- Skills Overview — Learn about grain bids, weather, and scale tickets in depth

title: Personality Presets
body:
Overview

Every FarmClaw agent has a personality preset that defines how it communicates, what it focuses on, and what it will and won't do. Think of presets as job descriptions for your AI assistants — they set the tone, expertise, and boundaries.

FarmClaw ships with five built-in presets plus a custom option. Each preset is carefully designed for its role, including explicit guardrails about topics the agent will not handle.

Available presets

General Advisor

The all-purpose farm assistant. If you're running a single agent, this is the default.

| Property | Details |
|----------|---------|
| Tone | Knowledgeable, no-nonsense, practical |
| Think of it as | A sharp neighbor who knows everything about markets, weather, and ag programs |
| Focus areas | Weather, grain markets, general operations, daily routines |
| Default skills | Grain Bids, Weather |
| Default cron jobs | Morning Weather, Daily Grain Bids |

Communication style:

- Direct and practical — no fluff
- Uses common agricultural terminology naturally (basis, carry, prevent plant)
- Always includes date/time stamps with price and weather data
- Uses Fahrenheit and inches
- Defaults to the farmer's local time zone
- Honest when it doesn't know something — won't guess on prices or forecasts

Daily routines (when cron jobs are enabled):

- 6:00 AM — Morning weather briefing: today's forecast, wind, precipitation, severe weather alerts
- 9:00 AM Mon-Fri — Daily cash grain bids from all configured elevators

The General Advisor is NOT a licensed financial advisor or commodity broker. It will never recommend specific trades. It presents market data, basis levels, and historical comparisons — you make your own decisions. It is also NOT an agronomist. It relays weather data and general best practices but recommends consulting your agronomist for specific crop recommendations.

---

Grain Specialist

A focused grain marketing agent that leads with numbers.

| Property | Details |
|----------|---------|
| Tone | Direct, data-driven, numbers-focused |
| Think of it as | A sharp market analyst who speaks farmer |
| Focus areas | Cash bids, basis levels, elevator comparisons, delivery periods, historical basis patterns |
| Default skills | Grain Bids |
| Default cron jobs | Daily Grain Bids |

Communication style:

- Leads with numbers — bid, basis, and date always come first
- Compares elevators side-by-side when relevant
- Flags significant basis changes (moves greater than 5 cents) proactively
- Uses standard grain marketing terminology (basis, carry, inverse, HTA, DP)
- Flags stale bids (older than 24 hours) clearly

How it handles common requests:

| You ask | It responds with |
|---------|-----------------|
| "What's corn at?" | Corn bids from ALL elevators, sorted best-to-worst |
| "Basis?" | Cash bid minus nearby futures for each crop at each elevator |
| "Compare elevators" | Side-by-side table: elevator, crop, bid, basis, delivery period |
| "Best bid for beans?" | Ranked soybean bids with basis and delivery terms |

The Grain Specialist is NOT a licensed commodity broker. It will never recommend specific trades or tell you when to sell. It presents data and comparisons — you decide. It always cites data sources and timestamps.

---

Weather & Field Ops

A cautious, detail-oriented weather specialist focused on field conditions.

| Property | Details |
|----------|---------|
| Tone | Cautious, detail-oriented, safety-first |
| Think of it as | Your weather-obsessed neighbor who always checks conditions twice |
| Focus areas | Forecasts, spray windows, GDD tracking, frost risk, severe weather, field accessibility |
| Default skills | Weather |
| Default cron jobs | Morning Weather |

Communication style:

- Leads with critical info: severe weather, frost risk, wind advisories
- Always uses Fahrenheit and inches
- Errs on the side of caution for marginal spray conditions
- Explicitly states when forecast confidence is low
- Clearly distinguishes between current observations and forecast data

How it handles common requests:

| You ask | It responds with |
|---------|-----------------|
| "What's the weather?" | Today's forecast + 3-day outlook, with any alerts highlighted |
| "Spray window?" | Next 48h hourly breakdown with GO/NO-GO markers (wind, temp, humidity, rain) |
| "GDD?" | Current accumulated growing degree days for your crops |
| "Can I get in the field?" | Recent precip, estimated soil conditions, next rain chance |
| "Frost risk?" | Overnight lows for next 5 days, anything below 32F flagged |

Spray window thresholds:

| Condition | GO threshold |
|-----------|-------------|
| Wind speed | Below 10 mph |
| Temperature | 50-85F |
| Rain-free window | 4+ hours |
| Humidity | Below 85% |

The Weather & Field Ops agent is NOT an agronomist. It relays weather data, GDD calculations, and general spray condition guidelines, but recommends consulting your agronomist for specific product application decisions. It always cites NWS as the data source.

---

Scale Ticket Clerk

A precise, efficient agent built for fast ticket logging.

| Property | Details |
|----------|---------|
| Tone | Precise, efficient, organized |
| Think of it as | Your bookkeeper who never loses a ticket |
| Focus areas | Ticket entry, tracking, reporting, delivery records |
| Default skills | Scale Tickets |
| Default cron jobs | None |

Communication style:

- Concise — farmers logging tickets are often in the truck
- Always confirms data before saving, showing a formatted summary
- Asks when in doubt about a number — never guesses weights or moisture
- Uses standard grain terminology: gross, tare, net, moisture, test weight, dockage
- Auto-calculates net weight when gross and tare are provided
- Converts between pounds and bushels using standard conversion factors

Three entry methods:

| Method | How it works | Saved as |
|--------|-------------|----------|
| Photo OCR | Post a photo of your scale ticket. The agent reads all visible fields using vision AI. | source: ocr |
| Voice / text | Type or dictate naturally: "47k of corn, 17.6 moisture, home field, taking to CHS" | source: voice |
| Guided entry | Say "new ticket" and the agent walks through fields step by step | source: manual |

Guided entry groups:

1. Date (defaults to today), Elevator, Crop
2. Gross weight, Tare weight (net auto-calculated)
3. Moisture %, Test weight, Dockage % (skippable)
4. Price/bu, Grade, Truck , Load , Field, Notes (skippable)

Photo OCR is the fastest way to log tickets. Snap a picture of the printed scale ticket from your phone and post it in the tickets channel. The Ticket Clerk extracts all the fields and shows you a summary to confirm before saving.

The Scale Ticket Clerk is NOT a grain marketing advisor. It logs deliveries. If you ask about prices or marketing strategy, it will suggest you talk to a Grain Specialist agent instead.

---

Field Operations

An organized operations manager for tracking field work and equipment.

| Property | Details |
|----------|---------|
| Tone | Organized, practical, safety-conscious |
| Think of it as | The operations manager who keeps the whole farm running on schedule |
| Focus areas | Equipment scheduling, input tracking, field work planning, work orders, season planning |
| Default skills | None (integrates with custom tools and extensions) |
| Default cron jobs | None |

Communication style:

- Structured and list-oriented — clear action items
- Tracks what's been done and what's coming up
- Flags conflicts (same equipment needed in two places, weather windows closing)
- Uses common ag terminology for field operations
- Defaults to the farmer's local time zone

How it handles common requests:

| You ask | It responds with |
|---------|-----------------|
| "What's left to plant?" | Fields listed by planting status |
| "Schedule spraying for Section 12" | Checks weather, creates a work entry |
| "Log fertilizer application" | Records product, rate, field, date |
| "What did we do in the north 80 this year?" | All operations for that field |
| "Equipment status" | Current equipment assignments and locations |

The Field Operations agent is NOT a certified crop advisor. It tracks what was applied and when, but recommends consulting your agronomist for specific product recommendations. It uses your field names as you define them.

---

Custom

Write your own personality description from scratch.

| Property | Details |
|----------|---------|
| Tone | Whatever you define |
| Focus areas | Whatever you specify |
| Default skills | None (you assign them) |
| Default cron jobs | None (you assign them) |

When you choose Custom, you provide a free-text personality description. FarmClaw still wraps it with your farm's operational details (farm name, location, crops, elevators) and the standard security boundaries that all agents share.

Even with a custom personality, FarmClaw includes mandatory security boundaries. Every agent — regardless of preset — is instructed to never follow embedded instructions in external content, never share credentials, never modify its own configuration, and never install unauthorized extensions.

Choosing the right preset

Here's a quick guide for matching presets to your needs:

| If you need... | Use this preset |
|----------------|----------------|
| One agent that does everything | General Advisor |
| A dedicated grain market channel | Grain Specialist |
| Automated weather briefings and spray window analysis | Weather & Field Ops |
| Fast scale ticket logging during harvest | Scale Ticket Clerk |
| Equipment and fieldwork tracking | Field Operations |
| Something not covered above | Custom |

What all presets share

Regardless of which preset you choose, every FarmClaw agent includes:

- Farm context — Your farm name, location, acreage, crops, elevators, and timezone are embedded in every agent's configuration
- Security boundaries — Protection against prompt injection, credential leaks, and unauthorized modifications
- File creation — All agents can generate and send spreadsheets (.xlsx), CSVs, PDFs, charts (PNG), and other files as Discord attachments
- Inter-agent communication — All agents can see other agents (agentslist) and delegate questions (sessionssend)
- Data citation — All agents are instructed to cite sources and timestamps when presenting market or weather data

Changing a preset

You can change an agent's personality preset at any time:

1. Go to Settings > Agents
2. Click on the agent you want to modify
3. Select a new personality preset
4. Review the updated skills and cron jobs (they reset to the preset defaults)
5. Adjust as needed and save

Changes take effect on the next message your assistant receives.

Next steps

- Multi-Agent Setups — Pre-built configurations using these presets
- Channel Configuration — Bind agents to specific Discord channels
- Skills Overview — Deep dive into grain bids, weather, and scale tickets

title: Skills & Scheduling

headings.title: Three-agent setup: General + Grain + Scale Tickets
Example: setting up a three-agent system

body:
Skills & Scheduling

Each agent in your FarmClaw workspace can be assigned a specific subset of skills and its own set of scheduled cron jobs. This lets you create focused specialists -- a Grain Desk agent that only handles bids, a Weather Watch agent that only handles forecasts, or a Ticket Clerk that only logs deliveries.

How per-agent skills work

FarmClaw has two levels of skill configuration:

1. Global skill toggle -- Enables or disables a skill for your entire workspace (Settings > Skills).
2. Per-agent assignment -- Determines which of the globally-enabled skills each agent can use (Settings > Agents > [Agent]).

A skill must be enabled globally before it can be assigned to any agent. If you disable a skill globally, it is removed from all agents automatically.

| Level | Where to configure | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| Global | Settings > Skills | Controls whether the skill is available at all |
| Per-agent | Settings > Agents > [Agent] | Controls which agents can use the skill |

Think of it as two gates. The global toggle is the first gate -- if the skill is off globally, no agent can use it. The per-agent assignment is the second gate -- even if a skill is on globally, only agents with it assigned will use it.

Assigning skills to an agent

1. Go to Settings > Agents.
2. Select the agent you want to configure.
3. Under Skills, toggle each skill on or off for that agent.
4. Click Save Changes.

Available skills

| Skill | ID | What it provides |
|---|---|---|
| Grain Bids | farm-grain-bids | Elevator website scraping for cash grain prices |
| Weather | farm-weather | NWS forecasts, spray windows, GDD tracking |
| Scale Tickets | farm-scale-tickets | Delivery logging via photo, voice, or guided entry |

Personality preset defaults

When you create a new agent and select a personality preset, FarmClaw auto-selects a recommended set of skills. You can change these after creation.

| Personality preset | Default skills |
|---|---|
| General Advisor | Grain Bids, Weather |
| Grain Specialist | Grain Bids |
| Weather & Field Ops | Weather |
| Scale Ticket Clerk | Scale Tickets |
| Field Operations | (none) |
| Custom | (none) |

Channel bindings

Each agent can be bound to a specific Discord channel. When a message comes in on a bound channel, only the assigned agent responds.

- Bound channels -- The assigned agent handles all messages in that channel.
- Unbound channels -- The default agent handles messages in any channel that is not explicitly bound to another agent.
- DMs -- The default agent always handles direct messages to the bot.

This means you can set up a grain-bids channel bound to your Grain Desk agent and a weather channel bound to your Weather Watch agent. General questions in farm-general (or any unbound channel) go to your default General Advisor.

Every workspace must have exactly one default agent. This agent handles DMs and any channel that is not bound to a specific agent. It is usually your General Advisor with all skills enabled.

Suggested multi-agent setups

FarmClaw provides pre-built agent configurations that you can apply with one click during setup. These are starting points -- you can customize them after applying.

Two-agent setup: General + Grain

| Agent | Preset | Skills | Cron jobs | Default |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Farm Assistant | General Advisor | Grain Bids, Weather, Scale Tickets | Morning Weather | Yes |
| Grain Desk | Grain Specialist | Grain Bids | Daily Grain Bids | No |

Best for operations that want focused grain market monitoring in a dedicated channel while keeping a general-purpose assistant for everything else.

Three-agent setup: General + Grain + Scale Tickets

| Agent | Preset | Skills | Cron jobs | Default |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Farm Assistant | General Advisor | Grain Bids, Weather | Morning Weather | Yes |
| Grain Desk | Grain Specialist | Grain Bids | Daily Grain Bids | No |
| Ticket Clerk | Scale Ticket Clerk | Scale Tickets | (none) | No |

Adds a dedicated scale ticket clerk. Useful during harvest when you are logging dozens of tickets per day and want a separate channel for delivery records.

Four-agent setup: Full Team

| Agent | Preset | Skills | Cron jobs | Default |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Farm Assistant | General Advisor | Grain Bids, Weather, Scale Tickets | (none) | Yes |
| Grain Desk | Grain Specialist | Grain Bids | Daily Grain Bids | No |
| Weather Watch | Weather & Field Ops | Weather | Morning Weather | No |
| Ticket Clerk | Scale Ticket Clerk | Scale Tickets | (none) | No |

Full specialist coverage. Each agent focuses on its area of expertise, and the general advisor handles anything that falls through the cracks.

Cron jobs (scheduled messages)

Cron jobs are automated messages that an agent sends on a schedule. They run in the agent's bound channel (or the default channel if the agent has no binding).

Available cron jobs

| Cron job | Schedule | Required skill | What it posts |
|---|---|---|---|
| Morning Weather | Daily at 6:00 AM | Weather | Full morning briefing: today's forecast, 3-day outlook, alerts, spray window assessment, ag notes |
| Daily Grain Bids | Mon--Fri at 9:00 AM | Grain Bids | Cash grain bids from all configured elevators, organized by commodity |

All times are in your farm's local timezone (Central for most states, Mountain for Montana).

Enabling cron jobs

1. Go to Settings > Agents.
2. Select the agent that should run the cron job.
3. Under Scheduled Jobs, toggle on the cron job.
4. Click Save Changes.

You can only enable a cron job on an agent that has the required skill assigned. For example, the Morning Weather cron job requires the Weather skill. If the Weather skill is not assigned to the agent, the Morning Weather toggle will not appear.

Personality preset defaults

When you create a new agent with a personality preset, FarmClaw auto-enables recommended cron jobs:

| Personality preset | Default cron jobs |
|---|---|
| General Advisor | Morning Weather, Daily Grain Bids |
| Grain Specialist | Daily Grain Bids |
| Weather & Field Ops | Morning Weather |
| Scale Ticket Clerk | (none) |
| Field Operations | (none) |
| Custom | (none) |

You can change these defaults after agent creation.

Inter-agent communication

When you have multiple agents, they can collaborate. If a farmer asks the Grain Desk about the weather, the Grain Desk can delegate the question to the Weather Watch agent using inter-agent messaging.

This happens automatically based on each agent's personality configuration:

- Grain Desk defers weather and scale ticket questions to the appropriate specialist.
- Weather Watch defers grain pricing and scale ticket questions.
- Ticket Clerk defers grain pricing and weather questions.
- General Advisor can handle most questions directly but will defer to specialists when they are available.

The agent always tells the farmer when it is passing a question to another assistant, so there is no confusion about who is responding.

When an agent delegates to another, you will see a message like "Let me pass that to Weather Watch -- they handle forecasts." The response then comes from the specialist agent.

Example: setting up a three-agent system

Here is a walkthrough of configuring a three-agent system from scratch.

1. Enable all skills globally.

Go to Settings > Skills and make sure Grain Bids, Weather, and Scale Tickets are all toggled on.

2. Create Agent 1: Farm Assistant (default).

- Personality: General Advisor
- Skills: Grain Bids, Weather
- Cron: Morning Weather
- Channel: (none -- handles unbound channels and DMs)
- Default: Yes

3. Create Agent 2: Grain Desk.

- Personality: Grain Specialist
- Skills: Grain Bids
- Cron: Daily Grain Bids
- Channel: grain-bids
- Default: No

4. Create Agent 3: Ticket Clerk.

- Personality: Scale Ticket Clerk
- Skills: Scale Tickets
- Cron: (none)
- Channel: scale-tickets
- Default: No

5. Save your changes.

Your three agents will come online, each responding in their designated channels. The Farm Assistant handles DMs and any channel not explicitly bound to another agent.

title: Billing & Usage

headings.title: What's included in the base subscription

body:
How billing works

FarmClaw uses a straightforward pricing model with two components:

1. Monthly base subscription — A fixed $50/month fee that covers your AI assistant and platform access
2. Metered API usage — Billed at cost based on the actual AI model tokens your assistant consumes

This structure keeps the base price low while ensuring you only pay for what you use.

What's included in the base subscription

Your $50/month subscription includes:

- Your AI assistant, available 24/7
- All three built-in skills (grain bids, weather, scale tickets)
- Multi-agent support with personality presets
- Built-in farm tools (scale tickets, grain bids, weather, memory)
- Access to the extension marketplace
- Dashboard with usage analytics
- Encrypted configuration storage

What counts as API usage

Every time your AI assistant processes a message — whether it's answering a grain bid question, generating a weather briefing, or reading a scale ticket photo — it uses tokens from the underlying AI model (Claude by Anthropic).

Token usage varies by task:

- Simple question/answer: 500–1,000 tokens
- Grain bid fetch with comparison: 2,000–4,000 tokens
- Scale ticket photo OCR: 3,000–5,000 tokens
- Morning weather briefing: 1,500–3,000 tokens

For a typical farm operation, metered usage runs $5–15/month depending on how actively you use the assistant.

Managing your billing

All billing is managed through Settings > Billing in your FarmClaw dashboard. From there you can:

- View your current plan and billing period
- See your metered usage breakdown
- Update your payment method
- Access invoices and billing history
- Cancel your subscription

Payments are processed securely through Stripe. FarmClaw never stores your credit card information directly.

title: Metered Usage
body:
How metering works

Every interaction with your AI assistant consumes tokens — the unit of measurement for AI model usage. FarmClaw tracks token usage daily and bills it as a separate line item on top of your $50/month subscription.

What are tokens?

Tokens are the building blocks of AI text processing. Roughly:

- 1 token ≈ 4 characters of English text
- 100 tokens ≈ 75 words
- A typical question and answer uses 500–2,000 tokens

Both the message you send and the response your assistant generates count toward token usage.

Usage by task

Different tasks consume different amounts of tokens:

| Task | Typical Token Usage |
|------|-------------------|
| Simple question/answer | 500–1,000 |
| Grain bid lookup (single elevator) | 1,500–2,500 |
| Grain bid comparison (multiple elevators) | 2,000–4,000 |
| Weather forecast | 1,000–2,000 |
| Morning weather briefing (cron) | 1,500–3,000 |
| Scale ticket — text entry | 1,000–2,000 |
| Scale ticket — photo OCR | 3,000–5,000 |
| Scale ticket — structured entry | 2,000–4,000 |
| Scale ticket query/report | 500–1,500 |

Typical monthly costs

For most farm operations, metered API usage runs $5–15/month:

| Usage Level | Description | Estimated Cost |
|-------------|-------------|---------------|
| Light | A few questions per day, automated briefings | $5–8/month |
| Moderate | Regular bid checks, ticket logging, weather queries | $8–12/month |
| Heavy | Frequent comparisons, multiple agents, lots of OCR | $12–20/month |

Tracking your usage

Dashboard

Your FarmClaw dashboard shows real-time usage analytics:

- Daily token consumption — Bar chart showing tokens used per day
- Cost breakdown — Running total for the current billing period
- Per-model breakdown — Token usage split by AI model
- Trend lines — See how your usage changes over time

Usage table

The apiusage table tracks daily metering per model:

- Date
- Model used
- Input tokens consumed
- Output tokens generated
- Estimated cost

Access your usage data anytime from Settings > Usage or the main dashboard.

Cost transparency

FarmClaw bills API usage at cost — we don't mark up token prices. You pay exactly what the underlying AI model charges, with full visibility into your usage.

The AI model powering your assistant is Claude by Anthropic. Current token pricing is displayed in your usage dashboard and updated if model pricing changes.

Reducing usage

If you want to keep costs down:

- Disable unused cron jobs — Automated briefings run daily even if you're not reading them
- Use fewer agents — Each agent in a multi-agent setup consumes tokens independently
- Be specific — Clear, direct questions use fewer tokens than vague ones
- Use text entry for scale tickets instead of photo OCR when possible — OCR uses significantly more tokens due to image processing

title: Spending Cap

headings.title: What happens when the cap is reached

body:
Spending Cap

The spending cap lets you set a maximum monthly budget for metered AI usage. Once your assistant reaches the cap, it pauses automatically -- protecting you from unexpected charges.

Setting a spending cap

1. Go to Settings > Billing (or Settings > Spending Cap)
2. Enter your monthly spending limit ($1--$1,000)
3. Toggle Auto-pause at cap to control whether your assistant stops when the limit is reached
4. Optionally set an alert email to receive spending notifications
5. Click Save

The cap applies to metered API usage only -- it does not affect your $50/month base subscription.

Spending alerts

FarmClaw sends alerts at three thresholds:

| Threshold | Severity | Notification |
|---|---|---|
| 50% of cap | Info | Dashboard notification |
| 80% of cap | Warning | Dashboard + email alert |
| 100% of cap | Critical | Dashboard + email alert |

FarmClaw also detects spending anomalies -- if today's usage is more than 3x your daily average (and over $0.50), you'll receive a warning.

Spending is checked automatically every 4 hours.

What happens when the cap is reached

When auto-pause is enabled and your metered usage reaches the spending cap:

- Your assistant stops responding to new messages in Discord
- Scheduled jobs (morning weather, daily grain bids) are paused
- Your dashboard shows a clear indicator that the cap is active

When the spending cap is reached with auto-pause enabled, your assistant will not respond until the next billing period starts or you increase the cap. Make sure your cap is set high enough for your normal usage.

Resuming after a pause

Your assistant resumes automatically when you:

- Increase the cap above your current month's spending -- the pause clears immediately
- Remove the cap -- set the value to $0 or clear the field
- Wait for the next billing period -- usage resets at the start of each month

Recommended cap levels

| Usage Level | Recommended Cap |
|---|---|
| Light (a few questions/day) | $15--20/month |
| Moderate (regular daily use) | $25--30/month |
| Heavy (frequent use, multiple agents, OCR) | $40--50/month |

If you're unsure, start with a $30 cap and adjust based on your actual usage.

Monitoring usage

Track your spending in real time from the dashboard:

- Current period usage -- How much you've spent so far this billing period
- Daily breakdown -- Token usage and cost per day
- Cap utilization -- Progress bar showing how close you are to your cap

See Metered Usage for details on what drives costs and how to reduce usage.

What's next

- Metered Usage -- Understand token costs and usage patterns
- Subscription -- Manage your base subscription

title: Subscription
body:
Plan details

FarmClaw offers a single subscription plan:

| Feature | Details |
|---------|---------|
| Monthly price | $50.00 |
| Billing cycle | Monthly |
| Payment processor | Stripe |
| Cancellation | Anytime, effective end of billing period |

What you get

Your subscription activates your FarmClaw workspace, which includes:

- Built-in farm tools — Scale tickets, grain bids, weather forecasts, memory
- Three built-in skills — Grain bids, weather forecasts, scale tickets
- Multi-agent support — Up to 4 specialized agents
- Extension marketplace — Access to additional capabilities
- Dashboard — Usage analytics, settings management
- Automated scheduling — Cron jobs for morning briefings and daily bid updates

Activating your subscription

During onboarding, you'll be redirected to Stripe's secure checkout:

1. Click "Subscribe" on the activation prompt
2. Enter your payment information on the Stripe checkout page
3. Complete the purchase
4. You're redirected back to FarmClaw to continue onboarding

All payment processing is handled by Stripe. FarmClaw never sees or stores your credit card number. Stripe is PCI Level 1 certified — the highest level of payment security.

Managing your subscription

Access your subscription settings at Settings > Billing:

Update payment method

Click "Manage Billing" to open the Stripe customer portal where you can update your card on file, switch payment methods, or add a backup payment method.

View invoices

The Stripe portal shows your complete billing history with downloadable PDF invoices for each billing period.

Cancel your subscription

You can cancel anytime from the Stripe portal. Your workspace will remain active until the end of your current billing period. After that:

- Your AI assistant goes offline
- Your data (farm profile, scale tickets, configuration) is retained for 30 days
- You can reactivate within 30 days to restore everything

After 30 days of cancellation, your workspace data including scale ticket records may be permanently deleted. Export any important data before canceling.

title: Channel Configuration
body:
Overview

Channel configuration lets you control which AI agent responds in which Discord channel. This is how you keep conversations organized — grain questions go to the grain expert, weather questions go to the weather agent, and your general assistant handles everything else.

If you're running a single agent (the default), it responds in all channels and DMs. Channel bindings only matter when you have multiple agents set up.

How channel bindings work

Each agent in your FarmClaw workspace can be bound to one or more specific Discord channels. When a message comes in on a bound channel, only the assigned agent responds.

Here are the key rules:

- Bound channels — Only the assigned agent responds in that channel
- Unbound channels — The default agent handles any channel without an explicit binding
- Direct messages — Always handled by the default agent
- One agent per channel — Each channel can only have one agent bound to it

Every FarmClaw workspace must have exactly one agent marked as the default. This agent is the catch-all — it handles DMs and any channel that isn't explicitly bound to another agent.

Example setup

Here's a common three-agent configuration with channel bindings:

| Discord Channel | Bound Agent | Personality | What it handles |
|----------------|-------------|-------------|-----------------|
| general | Farm Assistant (default) | General Advisor | Daily questions, broad farm topics, delegation |
| grain-desk | Grain Desk | Grain Specialist | Cash bids, basis, elevator comparisons |
| weather | Farm Assistant (default) | General Advisor | Unbound channel, handled by default agent |
| scale-tickets | Ticket Clerk | Scale Ticket Clerk | Logging and tracking deliveries |
| DMs | Farm Assistant (default) | General Advisor | Private questions, always default agent |

In this example, grain-desk and scale-tickets are bound to specialist agents. general and weather have no explicit binding, so the default Farm Assistant handles them.

You don't need to bind every channel on day one. Start with your default agent handling everything, then add specialist agents and channel bindings as you see which topics come up most.

Configuring channel bindings

From the dashboard

1. Go to Settings > Channels in your FarmClaw dashboard
2. You'll see a list of your Discord server's channels
3. For each channel, select which agent should handle it from the dropdown
4. Channels left as "Default" will be handled by your default agent
5. Click Save

Changes apply to your assistant immediately.

When creating an agent

You can also assign a channel when creating or editing an agent:

1. Go to Settings > Agents
2. Create a new agent or edit an existing one
3. Under Channel Binding, select a Discord channel
4. Save the agent

How agents handle cross-channel requests

If someone asks a question in general that would be better handled by the Grain Desk agent in grain-desk, the default agent can delegate the question. With inter-agent communication enabled, agents can:

1. Recognize that a question falls outside their expertise
2. Use agentslist to see which specialists are available
3. Use sessionssend to pass the question to the right agent
4. Inform the farmer that they're delegating to the specialist

The response comes back in the same channel where the question was asked. The farmer doesn't need to switch channels.

You don't need to configure delegation rules. Each agent's personality preset includes guidance on when to defer to other agents. The General Advisor knows to pass grain marketing questions to the Grain Specialist, weather questions to the Weather agent, and so on.

Channel configuration and security

Your Discord channel configuration is encrypted at rest in FarmClaw's database. Channel IDs and the mapping between channels and agents are stored using AES encryption, the same standard used for all sensitive data.

The configuration is decrypted at runtime when your assistant needs to connect to Discord.

Common patterns

Single agent (default)

No channel configuration needed. Your one agent handles everything everywhere.

Two agents: General + Grain

| Channel | Agent |
|---------|-------|
| grain-desk | Grain Desk |
| Everything else | Farm Assistant (default) |

Three agents: General + Grain + Tickets

| Channel | Agent |
|---------|-------|
| grain-desk | Grain Desk |
| scale-tickets | Ticket Clerk |
| Everything else | Farm Assistant (default) |

Four agents: Full Team

| Channel | Agent |
|---------|-------|
| grain-desk | Grain Desk |
| weather | Weather Watch |
| scale-tickets | Ticket Clerk |
| Everything else | Farm Assistant (default) |

Next steps

- AI Agents — Learn about multi-agent setups
- Multi-Agent Setups — Pre-built 2, 3, and 4-agent configurations
- Personality Presets — How agent personalities work

title: Discord Integration
body:
Why Discord?

FarmClaw delivers your AI assistant through Discord because it's the best fit for how farmers actually work:

- Mobile-first — Discord runs on your phone, tablet, and desktop. Ask for grain bids from the combine cab or check spray windows from the pickup.
- Already familiar — Many farmers and farm families already use Discord. No new app to learn.
- Multi-channel — Organize conversations by topic. Put grain talk in one channel, weather in another, scale tickets in a third.
- Threaded conversations — Keep discussions organized without losing context. Ask a follow-up question in a thread without cluttering the main channel.
- File sharing built in — Your assistant can send spreadsheets, charts, and PDFs directly in the conversation. Download a bid comparison Excel file right to your phone.
- Notifications you control — Set up push notifications for the channels that matter. Get alerted for morning weather but mute the grain desk on weekends.

One server, one workspace

Each FarmClaw workspace connects to one Discord server. When you sign up and complete onboarding, the FarmClaw bot joins your server and is ready to respond to messages.

If you don't already have a Discord server, you'll create one during setup. If you have an existing server for your operation, you can add the FarmClaw bot to it.

What you can configure

FarmClaw gives you control over how your assistant operates within Discord:

Bot setup

Add the FarmClaw bot to your Discord server during onboarding with a single click. No bot tokens or Developer Portal steps needed. See the Server Setup guide for step-by-step instructions.

Channel configuration

Map specific AI agents to specific Discord channels. For example, bind your Grain Specialist to grain-desk and your Weather agent to weather. Any channel without an explicit binding is handled by your default agent. See Channel Configuration for details.

Agent bindings

If you're running a multi-agent setup, each agent can be assigned to one or more channels. This keeps conversations focused — your Grain Specialist only responds in the grain channel, your Scale Ticket Clerk handles the tickets channel, and your general Farm Assistant covers everything else plus DMs.

Learn more about agents in the AI Agents section.

How it works in practice

Once your workspace is set up, using FarmClaw is as simple as typing a message in Discord:

Your assistant responds in the same channel, with formatted tables, data citations, and timestamps. It can also generate and attach files — spreadsheets, charts, PDFs — directly in the conversation.

Anything you can do from the Discord desktop app, you can do from the mobile app. FarmClaw responds the same way regardless of how you connect.

Next steps

- Server Setup — Add the FarmClaw bot to your Discord server
- Channel Configuration — Map agents to specific channels
- AI Agents — Learn about multi-agent setups and personality presets

title: Server Setup

headings.title: Step 2: Add FarmClaw to your Discord server
How to find your Server ID
Can't add the bot to my server

body:
Overview

This guide walks you through creating a Discord server and connecting it to FarmClaw. The entire process takes about 5 minutes.

You'll need:

- A Discord account (free at discord.com)
- Access to your FarmClaw onboarding wizard or Settings page

Step 1: Create a Discord server

If you already have a Discord server for your farm, skip to Step 2.

1. Open Discord (desktop app or web)
2. Click the + button in the server list on the left sidebar
3. Select Create My Own
4. Choose For me and my friends (or For a club or community if you prefer)
5. Name your server -- something like "Smith Farms" or "Prairie View Ag"
6. Click Create

Your server is ready. You can customize it later with channels, roles, and permissions.

Consider creating channels that match your workflow: general, grain-desk, weather, scale-tickets. You can bind different AI agents to each channel later.

Step 2: Add FarmClaw to your Discord server

FarmClaw uses a single shared bot -- you don't need to create your own bot application or manage any tokens.

During onboarding

On the Discord Connection step of the onboarding wizard, click Add FarmClaw to Discord. This opens a standard Discord OAuth authorization page where you select your server and grant the bot the permissions it needs.

Alternatively, you can paste your Discord Server ID directly if you prefer.

After onboarding

If you need to connect a different server later:

1. Go to Settings > Discord in your FarmClaw dashboard
2. Click Add FarmClaw to Discord or enter your Server ID
3. Click Save

How to find your Server ID

If you're pasting a Server ID manually:

1. Open Discord and right-click your server name
2. Click Copy Server ID (you may need to enable Developer Mode in Discord settings first: User Settings > Advanced > Developer Mode)
3. Paste the ID into FarmClaw

Bot permissions

The FarmClaw bot requests these permissions when you add it to your server:

| Permission | Purpose |
|------------|---------|
| Send Messages | Respond to your questions and post updates |
| Read Message History | Provide context in threaded conversations |
| Embed Links | Format responses with rich embeds (tables, cards) |
| Attach Files | Send spreadsheets, charts, and PDFs |
| Read Messages / View Channels | See messages in the channels it's assigned to |

These are the minimum permissions needed. FarmClaw does not request administrator access or any permissions beyond what's listed above.

Once you complete onboarding, your assistant is ready. The FarmClaw bot will appear online in your Discord server. Send it a message to verify everything is working.

Troubleshooting

Bot shows offline

- Verify your workspace is active on the FarmClaw dashboard
- Check that the FarmClaw bot hasn't been kicked from the server -- re-add it from Settings > Discord

Bot doesn't respond to messages

- Make sure the bot has Read Messages/View Channels and Send Messages permissions in the channel you're testing
- If using channel bindings, make sure the channel you're in is bound to an agent or is handled by the default agent
- Try messaging the bot in a DM to rule out channel permission issues

Can't add the bot to my server

- You need the Manage Server permission in Discord to add bots. Ask a server admin if you don't have this permission.
- If the authorization page shows an error, try logging out of Discord in your browser and logging back in.

Next steps

- Channel Configuration -- Map agents to specific Discord channels
- AI Agents -- Set up multiple agents with different specialties

title: Extensions
body:
Extensions

The FarmClaw extension system lets you expand your AI assistant's capabilities beyond the three built-in skills. Extensions are installable packages that add new skills, plugins, connectors, MCP servers, and tool packs to your workspace.

Extension types

Extensions come in five types, each serving a different purpose:

| Type | What it does | Example |
|---|---|---|
| skill | Adds a new capability or workflow to your assistant | A livestock price skill, a soil sampling analyzer |
| plugin | Modifies or enhances existing assistant behavior | A response formatter, a conversation logger |
| connector | Integrates with an external service or API | A John Deere Operations Center bridge, a DTN feed |
| mcpserver | Adds a new MCP server for tool access | A custom database connector, a cloud storage bridge |
| toolpack | Bundles multiple related tools together | A crop planning toolkit, a financial analysis suite |

Source types

Extensions can come from five different sources. Each source type has different trust and verification characteristics.

| Source | Description | Supports verification |
|---|---|---|
| internal | First-party extensions built and maintained by FarmClaw | No (implicitly trusted) |
| local | Extensions bundled directly with the platform | No (implicitly trusted) |
| clawhub | Third-party packages hosted on the FarmClaw marketplace | Yes |
| npm | Extensions published to the Node package registry | Yes |
| git | Extensions hosted in Git repositories, pinned by catalog reference | Yes |

Your account has a trust policy that determines which source types are allowed and which require verification before installation. By default, all sources are allowed, and npm, git, and clawhub sources require verified status. See Installing Extensions for details.

How extensions work

Extensions are managed through a catalog-and-installation model:

1. Catalog -- FarmClaw maintains a catalog of available extensions. Each catalog entry has a slug, type, source, description, and one or more versioned releases.
2. Installation -- When you install an extension, FarmClaw creates an installation record tied to your customer account and workspace. The installation tracks which version is active and the current health status.
3. Operations -- Install, upgrade, rollback, and remove actions are queued as operations. Each operation is tracked with a status (pending, inprogress, completed, failed, canceled) so you can monitor progress.
4. Health monitoring -- Installed extensions are continuously monitored. Each installation reports a health status: healthy, degraded, unhealthy, or unknown.

Versioned installations

Every extension in the catalog has one or more published versions. When you install an extension, you get the latest version by default. Each version includes:

- A semantic version number
- Release notes describing what changed
- A manifest with configuration and dependency information
- An optional SHA-256 checksum for integrity verification

You can pin an installation to a specific version to prevent automatic upgrades, or leave it unpinned to receive new versions through the rollout system.

Managing extensions

Extensions are managed through the FarmClaw dashboard at Settings > Extensions. The panel provides three sections:

- Trust Policy -- Configure which source types are allowed and which require verification.
- Installed -- View your active installations with status, health, and version info. Queue rollbacks if needed.
- Catalog -- Browse available extensions, see verification status, and install or upgrade with one click.

If an extension update causes problems, you can queue a rollback from the Installed section to revert to the previous version.

Extension lifecycle

An extension installation moves through the following statuses over its lifetime:

| Status | Meaning |
|---|---|
| pending | Installation or upgrade has been queued but not yet started |
| installing | The operation is actively running |
| installed | The extension is active and operational |
| failed | The installation or upgrade encountered an error |
| rollbackpending | A rollback has been queued |
| rolledback | Successfully reverted to a previous version |
| removed | The extension has been uninstalled |

What's next

- Marketplace -- Browse and discover available extensions
- Installing Extensions -- Trust policies, installation flow, health monitoring, and rollouts
- MCP Servers -- Built-in tool servers for filesystem, database, and memory access

title: Installing Extensions
body:
Installing Extensions

This guide covers the complete extension installation workflow: configuring your trust policy, installing extensions from the catalog, monitoring health, and understanding the staged rollout system for updates.

Trust policies

Every FarmClaw account has an extension trust policy that controls which extensions can be installed. The trust policy has two components:

Allowed source types

The allowedsourcetypes list determines which source types you permit at all. By default, all five source types are allowed:

- internal -- FarmClaw first-party extensions
- local -- Extensions bundled with the platform
- clawhub -- FarmClaw marketplace packages
- npm -- Node package registry packages
- git -- Git repository extensions

You must keep at least one source type allowed at all times.

Verification requirements

The requireverifiedsourcetypes list determines which allowed sources must be verified before installation. By default, three source types require verification:

- npm
- git
- clawhub

Internal and local sources do not support verification -- they are implicitly trusted because FarmClaw or your deployment controls them.

Out of the box, your trust policy allows all source types and requires verification for npm, git, and clawhub. This provides a balanced security posture: first-party and local extensions are always trusted, while third-party sources must be verified.

Configuring your trust policy

1. Go to Settings > Extensions.
2. In the Trust Policy section, toggle each source type on or off under the Allowed column.
3. For source types that support verification (clawhub, npm, git), toggle Require verified on or off.
4. Click Save Policy.

The policy takes effect immediately for all future installation requests.

| Source type | Can be allowed | Can require verification |
|---|---|---|
| internal | Yes | No (implicitly trusted) |
| local | Yes | No (implicitly trusted) |
| clawhub | Yes | Yes |
| npm | Yes | Yes |
| git | Yes | Yes |

If you set a source type to require verification but then disallow that source entirely, the verification requirement is automatically removed. Verified-required source types must also be in your allowed list.

How trust evaluation works

When you attempt to install an extension, FarmClaw evaluates it against your trust policy in two steps:

1. Source type check -- Is the extension's source type in your allowedsourcetypes? If not, the install is blocked.
2. Verification check -- Is the source type in your requireverifiedsourcetypes? If yes, is the extension marked as verified? If it requires verification and is not verified, the install is blocked.

If both checks pass, the installation proceeds.

Installation flow

Installing an extension from the catalog follows this sequence:

Step 1: Select an extension

Browse the Marketplace and find the extension you want to install. Click Install on the extension card.

Step 2: Trust policy check

FarmClaw automatically evaluates the extension against your trust policy. If the extension is blocked, you receive an error message explaining the reason (e.g., "source type 'npm' is blocked by trust policy" or "extension is unverified for source type 'clawhub'").

Step 3: Version resolution

FarmClaw resolves the target version. By default, this is the latest published version. If you are upgrading an existing installation, the latest version that is newer than your current version is selected.

You can optionally pin an installation to a specific version. Pinned installations do not receive automatic upgrades through the rollout system.

Step 4: Operation queued

FarmClaw creates an installation record (or updates an existing one) and queues an operation. The operation tracks the installation's progress:

| Operation field | Description |
|---|---|
| operationtype | install for new installations, upgrade for existing ones |
| scopetype | workspace if tied to a specific workspace, customer if account-wide |
| status | Starts as pending, progresses to inprogress, then completed or failed |

Step 5: Execution

The queued operation is picked up by FarmClaw's operation processor. It installs the extension package, configures it for your workspace, and updates the installation status.

Step 6: Confirmation

Once complete, the installation status changes to installed and the extension is active in your workspace. The health monitor begins tracking the extension's status.

Extension installations are processed asynchronously. After clicking Install, you can navigate away from the page. Check back on the Installed section to see the current status.

Health monitoring

Every installed extension is monitored for health. The health status appears as a badge next to each installation in the Installed section.

| Health status | Meaning |
|---|---|
| unknown | Health has not been evaluated yet (typical right after install) |
| healthy | The extension is operating normally |
| degraded | The extension is experiencing intermittent issues |
| unhealthy | The extension is failing consistently |

Health status is updated automatically. If an extension becomes unhealthy, you can queue a rollback to revert to the previous version.

Viewing health details

Each installed extension card shows:

- Install status -- Current installation state (installed, pending, failed, etc.)
- Health status -- Current health evaluation
- Last error -- If the extension has encountered an error, the most recent error message is displayed
- Version -- The currently installed version number
- Last updated -- When the installation was last modified

Rollbacks

If an extension update causes problems, you can revert to the previous version.

1. Go to Settings > Extensions.
2. Find the extension in the Installed section.
3. Click Queue Rollback.

FarmClaw queues a rollback operation that reverts the installation to the previous version. The installation status changes to rollbackpending and then to rolledback once complete.

A rollback changes the active version but does not delete the newer version from the catalog. You can upgrade again later when a fix is available.

Rollout system

When extension authors publish new versions, FarmClaw uses a staged rollout system to deploy updates safely across all installations. This prevents a bad update from affecting every customer at once.

Rollout rings

Updates are deployed in three rings, each progressively larger:

| Ring | Default batch size | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| canary | 1 instance | Early validation on a single installation |
| standard | 10 instances | Broader validation across a small group |
| delayed | 100 instances | Full rollout to remaining installations |

The rollout advances from canary to standard to delayed, pausing between rings to evaluate health metrics.

Gate policy

Between each ring, FarmClaw evaluates the update against health thresholds called the gate policy. If any threshold is exceeded, the rollout is halted and affected installations are rolled back.

| Metric | Default threshold | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Max failure rate | 10% | Maximum percentage of installations that can fail |
| Max P95 latency | 10,000 ms (10s) | Maximum 95th percentile response latency |
| Max timeout rate | 5% | Maximum percentage of installations that time out |
| Max hard error rate | 3% | Maximum percentage of installations with unrecoverable errors |

If the gate policy thresholds are exceeded during a rollout, FarmClaw automatically halts the rollout and rolls back affected installations. This protects your workspace from bad updates.

Rollout statuses

A rollout progresses through these statuses:

| Status | Meaning |
|---|---|
| pending | Rollout has been created but not started |
| inprogress | Currently deploying to the active ring |
| paused | Manually paused by an administrator |
| completed | All rings deployed successfully |
| failed | A ring failed and the rollout was stopped |
| canceled | Manually canceled before completion |
| halted | Automatically stopped due to gate policy violation |

Per-target tracking

Each installation targeted by a rollout has its own tracking record with:

- Ring assignment -- Which ring the installation belongs to
- Status -- pending, inprogress, completed, failed, skipped, or rolledback
- Attempt count -- How many times the update has been attempted
- Latency -- Measured response latency in milliseconds
- Timeout count -- Number of timeout events
- Hard error count -- Number of unrecoverable errors
- Error message -- Details if the target failed

Pinning versions

If you want to stay on a specific version and opt out of automatic rollouts:

1. Install the extension and note the version number.
2. The installation's pinnedversion field locks it to that version.

Pinned installations are skipped by the rollout system. To receive updates again, install the latest version manually, which clears the pin.

What's next

- Marketplace -- Browse and discover available extensions
- Extensions Overview -- Extension types, sources, and lifecycle
- Skills -- Built-in capabilities that come with every workspace
- MCP Servers -- Tool servers for filesystem, database, and memory access

title: Marketplace
body:
Marketplace

The FarmClaw extension marketplace is the catalog of all available extensions you can add to your AI assistant. Browse by type, filter by source, and check verification status before installing.

Browsing the catalog

The extension catalog is accessible from Settings > Extensions in your FarmClaw dashboard. The Catalog section at the bottom of the page displays all active extensions with their key details.

Each extension entry in the catalog shows:

- Display name -- The human-readable name of the extension.
- Slug -- A unique machine-readable identifier (e.g., crop-planning-toolkit).
- Type -- The extension kind: skill, plugin, connector, mcpserver, or toolpack.
- Source type -- Where the extension comes from: internal, local, clawhub, npm, or git.
- Latest version -- The most recently published version number, if available.
- Verified status -- Whether the extension has been verified for its source type.

Verified extensions display a green verified badge. Unverified extensions display an amber unverified badge.

Filtering extensions

The catalog supports several filters to help you find what you need.

By type

Filter by extension type to narrow results to a specific category:

| Filter value | Shows |
|---|---|
| skill | Extensions that add new assistant capabilities |
| plugin | Extensions that modify or enhance existing behavior |
| connector | Extensions that integrate with external services |
| mcpserver | Extensions that add new MCP tool servers |
| toolpack | Extensions that bundle multiple related tools |

By source

Filter by source type to see extensions from a specific origin:

| Filter value | Description |
|---|---|
| internal | First-party extensions managed by FarmClaw |
| local | Extensions bundled directly with the platform |
| clawhub | Third-party packages from the FarmClaw marketplace |
| npm | Node package registry sources |
| git | Git repositories pinned by catalog reference |

By verification status

Toggle the verified filter to show only extensions that have passed verification for their source type. This is useful when your trust policy requires verification for certain sources.

By search

Use the search field to find extensions by name or slug. The search matches against the display name and slug fields.

Pagination

The catalog returns up to 20 items per page by default. You can request up to 100 items per page through the API. The dashboard handles pagination automatically, displaying results in a scrollable list.

The marketplace only displays extensions that have been marked as active in the catalog. Deprecated or deactivated extensions are hidden from the browsing view.

Source types explained

Understanding where an extension comes from helps you evaluate whether to trust and install it.

Internal

Internal extensions are built and maintained by the FarmClaw team. They are first-party packages that ship with the platform and are implicitly trusted. Internal extensions do not require verification because FarmClaw controls the source code and release process.

Local

Local extensions are bundled directly with the FarmClaw platform. These are typically custom extensions that have been built specifically for your deployment. Like internal extensions, they are implicitly trusted because FarmClaw controls the source.

ClawHub

ClawHub is the FarmClaw-hosted marketplace for third-party extensions. Developers can publish extension packages to ClawHub, where they go through a review and verification process. ClawHub extensions support verification -- you can require that only verified ClawHub packages are installable through your trust policy.

ClawHub extensions go through a review process before being listed in the catalog. If you want third-party capabilities with an extra layer of trust, prioritize ClawHub-sourced extensions.

NPM

NPM extensions are published to the Node package registry. These are standard npm packages that implement the FarmClaw extension interface. NPM sources support verification -- the catalog tracks whether a given npm package has been verified by the FarmClaw team.

Git

Git extensions are hosted in Git repositories and pinned by a catalog reference (commit hash, tag, or branch). This source type is common for extensions in active development or extensions maintained by a specific organization. Git sources support verification.

Installing from the catalog

Each extension in the catalog has an Install button (or Queue Upgrade if you already have it installed). Clicking the button queues an installation operation.

Before the installation proceeds, FarmClaw checks your trust policy:

1. Is the extension's source type in your allowedsourcetypes list?
2. If the source type is in your requireverifiedsourcetypes list, is the extension verified?

If either check fails, the installation is blocked and you receive an error message explaining why.

If you change your trust policy to disallow a source type, you will not be able to install new extensions from that source. Existing installations are not automatically removed, but you will not be able to upgrade them.

For the full installation workflow, trust policy configuration, and health monitoring details, see Installing Extensions.

What's next

- Installing Extensions -- Trust policies, installation flow, health monitoring, and staged rollouts
- Extensions Overview -- Extension types, lifecycle, and versioning
- MCP Servers -- Built-in tool servers available without the marketplace

title: Crop Selection

headings.title: How Crop Selection Affects Your Experience

body:
Crop Selection

FarmClaw supports 10 row crops commonly grown in the Upper Midwest. During onboarding, you select the crops you grow as part of your farm profile. You must select at least one crop, and you can select as many as apply to your operation.

Supported Crops and Bushel Weights

Each crop has a standard bushel weight used for weight-to-bushel conversions throughout the platform. These weights follow USDA standard bushel weights and are used whenever your AI assistant converts between pounds and bushels -- for example, when logging scale tickets or calculating delivery totals.

| Crop | Bushel Weight (lbs/bu) |
|---|---|
| Corn | 56 |
| Soybeans | 60 |
| Spring Wheat | 60 |
| Winter Wheat | 60 |
| Durum | 60 |
| Barley | 48 |
| Sunflowers | 24 |
| Canola | 50 |
| Dry Beans | 60 |
| Flax | 56 |

When you deliver grain and log a scale ticket, your assistant converts net weight to bushels using the formula:

Net Bushels = Net Weight (lbs) / Bushel Weight (lbs/bu)

For example, a 48,000-pound load of corn converts to 857.1 bushels (48,000 / 56). Your assistant always displays both pounds and bushels so you can verify the math.

How Crop Selection Affects Your Experience

Grain Bid Tracking

When you configure grain elevators in the elevator setup, your selected crops determine which commodities are tracked at each elevator. If you grow corn and soybeans, your assistant pulls corn and soybean bids from each elevator and ignores commodities you do not produce.

When you ask your assistant for grain bids, it returns prices only for your crops. This keeps bid reports clean and relevant rather than listing every commodity an elevator handles.

Scale Tickets

Your crop list is used in three ways during scale ticket entry:

- Photo OCR -- When you photograph a scale ticket, the assistant cross-references the commodity on the ticket against your crop list to select the correct bushel weight for conversion.
- Voice and text entry -- When you dictate a load ("48k of corn to CHS"), the assistant recognizes the crop name and applies the correct conversion factor.
- Structured entry -- When walking through ticket fields step by step, your assistant suggests your configured crops in a pick list rather than showing all 10 options.

Agent Configuration

Your crop selection influences how AI agent personality presets behave:

- Grain Specialist agents focus on market analysis and basis tracking for your specific commodities.
- General Advisor agents tailor agronomic recommendations to the crops you actually grow.
- Scale Ticket Clerk agents pre-configure their visible fields and suggested entries based on your crop mix.

Common Crop Abbreviations

Your assistant understands common shorthand when you talk about crops in Discord:

| What You Say | What It Means |
|---|---|
| beans | Soybeans |
| HRS, spring wheat | Spring Wheat |
| HRW, winter wheat | Winter Wheat |
| durum | Durum |
| sunnies, sunflowers | Sunflowers |
| corn | Corn |
| canola | Canola |
| barley | Barley |

Select every crop you might grow in a typical rotation. Even if you are not growing durum this year, having it in your crop list means your assistant is ready if you add it next season. There is no cost difference for selecting more crops.

Updating Your Crops

You can add or remove crops at any time from Settings > Farm Profile in your dashboard. Changes take effect on the next message your assistant receives.

If you remove a crop from your profile, any scale tickets already logged for that crop remain in your database. You can still query and export them. The crop simply will not appear in suggested lists going forward.

title: Elevator Configuration

headings.title: How the Grain Bids Skill Uses Your Elevators

body:
Elevator Configuration

Elevator configuration connects your FarmClaw assistant to the grain elevators you deliver to. Once configured, your assistant can pull real-time cash grain bids from each elevator's website, compare prices across locations, and help you decide where to haul.

You set up elevators during Step 2 of onboarding (Grain Marketing) and can update them at any time from your dashboard.

What Each Elevator Needs

Every elevator in your configuration has three pieces of information:

| Field | Required | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Name | Yes | A display name for the elevator (e.g., "CHS Fargo") |
| URL | Yes | The direct URL to the elevator's cash bid page. Must be a valid URL. |
| Crops | Yes | Which of your selected crops to track at this elevator. At least one crop must be selected per elevator. |

For best results, enter the URL of the elevator's cash bid page specifically, not their homepage. The grain bids skill navigates directly to this URL and scrapes the bid table. A direct bid page URL means faster, more reliable results.

You can configure up to 20 elevators per workspace. Most farmers configure 2-5 elevators representing their primary delivery options.

Preset Elevator Companies

FarmClaw includes one-click presets for major elevator companies operating in the Upper Midwest. When you select your state in the farm profile, the onboarding wizard shows presets relevant to your area. Tap a preset to add it with the correct name, URL, and your selected crops pre-filled.

Available Presets

| Company | Preset Name | States Served |
|---|---|---|
| CHS | CHS - Fargo | ND, MN |
| CHS | CHS - Sioux Falls | SD |
| ADM | ADM - Casselton | ND |
| Cargill | Cargill - Minneapolis | MN |
| AGP | AGP - Omaha | NE, IA |
| Columbia Grain | Columbia Grain - Great Falls | MT |
| Gavilon | Gavilon - Des Moines | IA, NE |

Presets by State

Here is what you will see based on your state selection:

| State | Available Presets |
|---|---|
| North Dakota (ND) | CHS - Fargo, ADM - Casselton |
| South Dakota (SD) | CHS - Sioux Falls |
| Minnesota (MN) | CHS - Fargo, Cargill - Minneapolis |
| Montana (MT) | Columbia Grain - Great Falls |
| Iowa (IA) | AGP - Omaha, Gavilon - Des Moines |
| Nebraska (NE) | AGP - Omaha, Gavilon - Des Moines |

The presets cover major regional elevators, but most farmers also deliver to local independent elevators or co-ops. Use presets for the big companies and add your local elevators as custom entries.

Adding a Custom Elevator

If your elevator is not in the preset list, you can add it manually:

1. Click Add custom elevator below the preset list.
2. Enter the elevator name (e.g., "Farmers Union - Cooperstown").
3. Enter the URL of their cash bid page.
4. Click Add Elevator.

Your selected crops are automatically assigned to custom elevators. You can adjust which crops to track per elevator from your settings after onboarding.

Finding the Right URL

To find the bid page URL for a local elevator:

1. Go to the elevator's website in your browser.
2. Navigate to their cash bids, grain prices, or market information page.
3. Copy the URL from your browser's address bar.
4. Paste it into the URL field in FarmClaw.

A few elevator websites require selecting a location from a dropdown before showing bids. FarmClaw's grain bid skill can interact with page elements like dropdowns and buttons when needed. If your bids are not loading correctly, check that the URL points to the page that shows the actual bid table, even if it requires a location selection.

How the Grain Bids Skill Uses Your Elevators

Once your elevators are configured, here is what happens behind the scenes:

1. On demand -- When you ask your assistant "what are grain bids?" or "check corn prices", it navigates to each configured elevator's bid page using a browser tool.
2. Scraping -- It takes a snapshot of the page, locates the bid table, and extracts cash prices, basis levels, delivery periods, and futures month references for your crops.
3. Presentation -- Bids are displayed in a clean, scannable format in your Discord channel, organized by commodity.
4. Comparison -- When you ask to compare elevators, your assistant fetches bids from all configured elevators and highlights the best price for each commodity.

Example output in Discord:

Your assistant never caches or remembers old prices. Every bid check navigates to the elevator website and pulls current data. Cash bids are typically updated by elevators once daily in the morning. If you check in the evening, the bids shown are likely from that morning's posting.

Understanding Basis

Basis numbers in bid results represent the difference between the local cash price and the corresponding futures contract price, expressed in cents per bushel:

- Negative basis (e.g., -35) means the cash price is 35 cents per bushel below the futures price. This is the most common scenario.
- Positive basis (e.g., +10) means the cash price is 10 cents above futures, which can occur during tight local supply.

Your assistant includes the futures contract month reference (e.g., "Mar" for March) so you can see which futures contract the basis is calculated against.

Managing Elevators After Onboarding

You can add, remove, or update elevators at any time:

1. Open your FarmClaw dashboard.
2. Go to Settings > Farm Profile or Settings > Skills > Grain Bids.
3. Add new elevators, remove ones you no longer deliver to, or update URLs.
4. Save your changes.
