When you save elevator changes, FarmClaw applies the updates to your assistant. Changes take effect on the next message.

Troubleshooting

| Issue | Solution |
|---|---|
| Bids not showing for an elevator | Verify the URL points to the actual bid page. Try opening it in your browser to confirm bids are visible. |
| Wrong crops showing in bids | Check that the correct crops are assigned to that elevator in your settings. |
| Elevator website changed | Elevator companies occasionally redesign their websites. If scraping stops working, your assistant will notify you and suggest checking the elevator's site directly. |
| Bids seem outdated | Most elevators update bids once in the morning. Evening checks show the most recent posted bids, which may be from the morning. |

title: Farm Profile
body:
Farm Profile

Your farm profile is the first thing you fill out during onboarding. It tells FarmClaw where you are, what you grow, and the size of your operation. Every other feature -- grain bids, weather, scale tickets, agent behavior -- builds on this information.

Required Fields

All fields in the farm profile are required. Here is what each one does and what the system expects.

Farm Name

Your farm or operation name. This is used throughout the dashboard and in your AI assistant's configuration so it can refer to your operation by name.

| Requirement | Detail |
|---|---|
| Type | Text |
| Min length | 1 character |
| Max length | 100 characters |
| Example | Johnson Family Farm |

State

FarmClaw currently serves six Upper Midwest states. Your state selection controls which elevator presets appear, which county list is shown, and which timezone is applied.

| Supported States |
|---|
| North Dakota (ND) |
| South Dakota (SD) |
| Minnesota (MN) |
| Montana (MT) |
| Iowa (IA) |
| Nebraska (NE) |

Your timezone is set automatically based on your state. Montana uses America/Denver (Mountain Time). All other supported states use America/Chicago (Central Time). This timezone is used for scheduling cron jobs like morning weather briefings and daily grain bid checks.

County

After selecting a state, you will see a list of counties for that state. Select the county where your primary operation is located. Your county helps the system narrow down location-specific data like weather stations and regional market conditions.

Primary Crops

Select at least one crop that you grow. Tap or click crops to toggle them on or off. Your crop selections affect:

- Grain bids -- Only bids for your selected crops are tracked from each elevator.
- Scale tickets -- Your assistant suggests your crops during ticket entry and uses the correct bushel weights for conversions.
- Agent recommendations -- Agent personality presets are tuned based on the commodities you produce.

See Crop Selection for the full list of supported crops and their bushel weights.

Total Acres

Enter the total number of acres in your operation. This must be a whole number (no decimals) and must be a positive value up to 100,000 acres.

| Requirement | Detail |
|---|---|
| Type | Integer |
| Minimum | 1 |
| Maximum | 100,000 |
| Example | 2500 |

Your total acreage is used by AI agents for contextual advice. For example, when discussing application rates, seed needs, or harvest logistics, your assistant knows the scale of your operation.

Timezone Reference

Your timezone is populated automatically and cannot be edited manually. It is derived from your state selection:

| State | Timezone |
|---|---|
| North Dakota (ND) | America/Chicago (Central) |
| South Dakota (SD) | America/Chicago (Central) |
| Minnesota (MN) | America/Chicago (Central) |
| Montana (MT) | America/Denver (Mountain) |
| Iowa (IA) | America/Chicago (Central) |
| Nebraska (NE) | America/Chicago (Central) |

Editing Your Farm Profile

You can update your farm profile at any time after onboarding.

1. Open your FarmClaw dashboard.
2. Go to Settings > Farm Profile.
3. Make your changes and click Save Changes.

When you save changes to your farm profile, FarmClaw applies the updates to your assistant. Changes take effect on the next message.

Validation Summary

| Field | Type | Required | Constraints |
|---|---|---|---|
| Farm Name | String | Yes | 1-100 characters |
| State | Enum | Yes | ND, SD, MN, MT, IA, NE |
| County | String | Yes | Must match selected state |
| Primary Crops | Array | Yes | At least 1 crop selected |
| Total Acres | Integer | Yes | Positive, max 100,000 |

title: Farm Setup
body:
Farm Setup

Before your FarmClaw AI assistant can start helping you, it needs to know the basics about your operation. Farm setup is the first step in onboarding and takes about five minutes. The information you provide here drives everything your assistant does -- from pulling the right grain bids to calculating bushel weights on scale tickets.

Farm setup covers three key areas:

Farm Profile

Your farm profile includes your farm name, state, county, crop selection, and total acreage. This is the foundation that personalizes your entire FarmClaw experience. Your state selection determines your timezone, the elevator presets available to you, and the weather data sources used for forecasts.

Set up your farm profile

Crop Selection

FarmClaw supports 10 row crops common to the Upper Midwest. The crops you select determine which commodities your assistant tracks for grain bids, which bushel weight conversions it uses for scale tickets, and how your agents are configured.

Choose your crops

Elevator Configuration

Connect the grain elevators you deliver to so your assistant can pull real-time cash bids. FarmClaw includes one-click presets for major elevator companies operating in your state, and you can add any custom elevator with a public bid page.

Configure your elevators

---

How It All Fits Together

Once you complete farm setup, FarmClaw uses your profile to:

- Personalize your AI agents. Your agents know your crops, your elevators, and your location. When you ask "what are corn bids today?", they know exactly where to look.
- Configure grain bid tracking. The grain bids skill scrapes your configured elevator websites daily and compares cash bids across locations.
- Set up scale ticket management. When you log a delivery, your assistant uses the correct bushel weights for your crops and suggests your configured elevators by name.
- Deliver relevant weather. Forecasts, spray windows, and growing degree day calculations are based on your farm's coordinates and timezone.

Everything in farm setup can be updated at any time from Settings > Farm Profile in your dashboard. Changes are applied to your assistant automatically.

What Comes Next

After completing farm setup, the onboarding wizard continues with:

1. Location & Weather -- Set your weather coordinates for forecasts and field conditions.
2. Discord Connection -- Connect your Discord server so your assistant has a place to live.
3. Review & Launch -- Confirm your configuration and set up your FarmClaw workspace.

title: Account Setup
body:
Magic link authentication

FarmClaw uses magic link authentication powered by Supabase Auth. There are no passwords to remember or manage.

How it works

1. Enter your email address on the login or signup page
2. Check your inbox for an email from FarmClaw
3. Click the login link in the email
4. You're signed in — the link is single-use and expires after a short time

Your session stays active as you use FarmClaw. The system automatically refreshes your session in the background, so you won't be logged out during active use.

Troubleshooting login

If you don't receive the magic link email:

- Check your spam, junk, or promotions folder
- Make sure you entered the correct email address
- Wait a minute and try again — there's a short cooldown between requests
- If using a corporate email, check with your IT team about email filtering

Activating your subscription

After your first login, you'll need to activate a subscription before you can use FarmClaw. The subscription is managed through Stripe.

Subscription details

| Item | Cost |
|------|------|
| Monthly base subscription | $50.00/month |
| Metered API usage | Billed at cost |

The base subscription covers platform access and your AI assistant. API usage (the AI model calls your assistant makes) is metered daily and billed separately based on actual token usage.

Checkout flow

1. Click "Subscribe" on the onboarding prompt
2. You'll be redirected to a Stripe checkout page
3. Enter your payment information
4. Complete the purchase
5. You'll be redirected back to FarmClaw to begin onboarding

Managing your subscription

After setup, you can manage your subscription at any time from Settings > Billing:

- View your current plan and usage
- Update payment method
- Access billing history and invoices
- Cancel or modify your subscription

The billing portal is provided by Stripe and handles all payment processing securely.

Account security

- All sessions are managed server-side with secure HTTP-only cookies
- API routes validate authentication on every request
- Row-Level Security (RLS) ensures you can only access your own data
- Admin operations require a separate admin role

title: Welcome to FarmClaw
body:
What is FarmClaw?

FarmClaw gives your farm a dedicated AI assistant that lives in your Discord server. Ask it for cash grain bids, weather forecasts, spray window assessments, and more — all in plain English, right from your phone or desktop.

Each FarmClaw workspace is a fully configured multi-agent system powered by Claude, Anthropic's AI model. You get real-time access to the information that matters during the growing season and harvest.

Who is it for?

FarmClaw is designed for row crop farmers in the Upper Midwest:

- North Dakota
- South Dakota
- Minnesota
- Montana
- Iowa
- Nebraska

Whether you're farming 500 acres of corn and soybeans or 10,000 acres of spring wheat and durum, FarmClaw scales to your operation.

What can it do?

FarmClaw comes with three core skills out of the box:

- Grain Bids — Scrapes your configured elevators for current cash bids, basis levels, and delivery periods. Compare prices across elevators instantly.
- Weather Forecasts — Pulls forecasts from the National Weather Service. Includes spray window analysis, growing degree day calculations, and automated morning briefings.
- Scale Tickets — Log grain deliveries via photo OCR, voice dictation, or guided entry. Track totals by crop, elevator, and date. Export to spreadsheets.

Beyond the built-in skills, the extension marketplace lets you add more capabilities as they become available.

How does it work?

1. Sign up and activate your $50/month subscription
2. Complete onboarding — set up your farm profile, elevators, weather location, and Discord server
3. Your workspace is created with your farm configuration
4. Start chatting in Discord — ask questions, get bids, log tickets, check weather

Your assistant is always available. Morning weather briefings and daily grain bid updates can be scheduled automatically.

Next steps

Ready to get started? Head to the Quick Start guide for the full walkthrough.

title: Quick Start
body:
Overview

This guide walks you through the entire setup process. By the end, you'll have a working AI assistant in your Discord server that can fetch grain bids, check weather, and log scale tickets.

Step 1: Create your account

Go to the FarmClaw signup page and enter your email address. We use magic link authentication -- no password required. Check your inbox for the login link and click it to access your dashboard.

Magic link emails occasionally land in spam or promotions folders. The email comes from FarmClaw and contains a one-time login link.

Step 2: Activate your subscription

After logging in, you'll be prompted to activate your subscription:

- $50/month base subscription
- Metered API usage billed at cost (typically $5--15/month depending on usage)
- Billing is handled securely through Stripe

Click "Subscribe" to complete checkout. You'll be redirected back to FarmClaw to begin onboarding.

Step 3: Complete the onboarding wizard

The onboarding wizard has three steps:

Operation

- Farm name -- A display name for your operation
- State -- Select your state (ND, SD, MN, MT, IA, or NE)
- County -- Your county name
- Primary crops -- Select from: Corn, Soybeans, Spring Wheat, Winter Wheat, Durum, Barley, Sunflowers, Canola, Dry Beans
- Acres -- Total acres in your operation (up to 100,000)
- Elevators -- Add the grain elevators you deliver to. We provide presets for major elevator companies: CHS, ADM, Cargill, AGP, Columbia Grain, and Gavilon with state-specific defaults.

Location

- Location -- Your town or zip code
- Coordinates -- Latitude and longitude for precise NWS forecasts
- Timezone -- Auto-populated based on your state

Discord

- Add FarmClaw to Discord -- Click the invite link to add the FarmClaw bot to your server, or paste your Discord Server ID directly. See the Discord Setup guide for details.

Step 4: Send your first message

Once onboarding is complete, your assistant is ready. Open your Discord server and try one of these:

Your AI assistant will respond in the channel. That's it -- you're up and running.

What's next?

- Farm Profile configuration -- Fine-tune your farm details
- Multi-agent system -- Set up specialized agents for different tasks
- Skills overview -- Learn about grain bids, weather, and scale tickets in depth

title: Connecting Services

headings.title: Dashboard did not update after completing OAuth
Revoking access from the service side

body:
Connecting Services

Each toolkit that requires authentication needs a one-time OAuth connection before your assistant can use it. This page covers the full connection lifecycle -- authorizing, monitoring, refreshing, and disconnecting services.

The OAuth flow

OAuth is the industry-standard protocol for granting third-party access to your accounts without sharing passwords. When you connect a service through FarmClaw, you go through the service provider's own login and consent screens. FarmClaw never sees or stores your service passwords.

Step by step

1. Initiate: In the Connected Accounts section, click Connect next to the service.
2. Confirm: A dialog shows what actions your assistant will have access to. Click Authorize [Service].
3. Authenticate: A popup window opens with the service's login page (e.g., Google's account chooser).
4. Consent: Review the permissions requested. Google, for example, shows exactly which scopes are being requested (read spreadsheets, send email, etc.).
5. Grant access: Click Allow or Grant Access on the service's consent page.
6. Complete: The popup shows "Connected" and closes automatically. The parent page updates with a green "Connected" badge.

What happens behind the scenes

When you click Authorize:

1. FarmClaw's API calls Composio to initiate an OAuth connection for your Composio entity.
2. Composio generates a redirect URL pointing to the service's OAuth authorization endpoint.
3. The popup navigates to this URL.
4. After you consent, the service redirects back to FarmClaw's callback URL.
5. The callback endpoint syncs your connection status from Composio and updates the database.
6. The popup sends a postMessage to the parent window, which triggers a UI refresh.

OAuth access tokens and refresh tokens are stored and managed by Composio, not FarmClaw. Composio handles token refresh automatically when tokens expire. You do not need to re-authorize unless the refresh token itself expires (which is rare for most services).

Which account to use

When the OAuth popup opens, you choose which account to connect. Some considerations:

Personal vs. shared accounts

| Scenario | Recommendation |
|---|---|
| Solo farmer | Use your personal Google/service account |
| Family operation | Use a shared account (e.g., farm@gmail.com) that all partners can access |
| Farm with employees | Use a dedicated farm account to avoid mixing personal and business data |

Multiple accounts

If you have multiple Google accounts (personal and farm), make sure to select the correct one during the OAuth flow. The account chooser screen lets you pick which account to authorize.

Each toolkit connects to one account. If you connect Google Sheets with your personal account and later want to switch to a farm account, disconnect the service first and then reconnect with the other account.

Managing connections

Viewing connection status

The Connected Accounts section in Settings > Integrations shows all selected toolkits that require authentication. Each entry displays:

- Service name -- The toolkit name (e.g., Google Sheets)
- Account identifier -- The email or username associated with the connection (e.g., farmer@gmail.com)
- Status badge -- Current connection state
- Action button -- Connect or Disconnect

Refreshing status

Click the Refresh button at the top of the Connected Accounts section to sync the latest connection status from Composio. This is useful if:

- You completed an OAuth flow but the status has not updated
- You want to verify a connection is still active
- You disconnected from the service provider's side (e.g., revoked access in Google settings)

Disconnecting a service

To disconnect a service:

1. Find the service in the Connected Accounts list.
2. Click the Disconnect button.
3. The connection is revoked immediately.

Disconnecting does the following:

- Removes the OAuth tokens from Composio
- Updates your connection status to "Disconnected"
- Your assistant can no longer access that service

Disconnecting does not deselect the toolkit. The toolkit remains selected in your configuration -- it just has no active connection. You can reconnect at any time.

When you disconnect a service, access is revoked immediately. There is no delay or grace period. Your assistant will get an authorization error if it tries to call that service's actions after disconnection.

Connection states

| State | Badge | Meaning | Action needed |
|---|---|---|---|
| Connected | Green | Active, working connection | None |
| Disconnected | Gray | No connection established | Click Connect |
| Expired | Amber | Token expired, needs re-auth | Click Connect to re-authorize |
| Pending | Blue | OAuth flow in progress | Complete the flow in the popup |

Expired connections

Most OAuth providers issue refresh tokens that Composio uses to automatically renew access. However, some scenarios cause expiration:

- You changed your service account password
- You revoked FarmClaw's access in the service's security settings
- The refresh token exceeded its maximum lifetime (uncommon)
- The service provider rotated their OAuth credentials (rare)

To fix an expired connection, click Connect again. The OAuth flow is the same as the initial connection.

Popup troubleshooting

Popup blocked

Most browsers block popups by default. If the OAuth popup does not appear:

1. Check for a popup notification in your browser's address bar (usually a small icon).
2. Click it and select "Always allow popups from this site."
3. Try clicking Connect again.

If popups remain blocked, FarmClaw falls back to a full-page redirect. You will be redirected away from the dashboard to complete the OAuth flow, then redirected back afterward.

Popup opened but stuck

If the popup opens but does not show the service's login page:

- Check your internet connection
- Try closing the popup and clicking Connect again
- Clear your browser cache for the service's domain (e.g., accounts.google.com)

Popup closed before completing

If you accidentally close the popup before granting consent:

- No connection is created
- No tokens are stored
- Click Connect again to restart the flow

Dashboard did not update after completing OAuth

If the popup showed "Connected" but the dashboard still shows "Disconnected":

1. Click the Refresh button in the Connected Accounts section
2. If that does not help, reload the entire page

Revoking access from the service side

You can also revoke FarmClaw's access directly from the service provider:

| Service | Where to revoke |
|---|---|
| Google (Sheets, Calendar, Gmail, Drive) | Google Account > Security > Third-party apps |
| Slack | Slack workspace settings > Manage apps |
| Notion | Notion settings > My connections |
| Todoist | Todoist settings > Integrations |
| Airtable | Airtable account settings > API |

After revoking from the service side, click Refresh in FarmClaw to update your connection status. The status will change to "Expired" or "Disconnected."

What's next

- Available Toolkits -- Full list of services and their actions
- Security and Privacy -- How credentials are stored and protected
- Setup Guide -- Complete setup walkthrough

title: Integrations

headings.title: What can your assistant do with integrations?

body:
Integrations

Integrations let your AI assistant connect to external services like Google Sheets, Gmail, Google Calendar, Slack, and hundreds more. Instead of being limited to skills and local tools, your assistant can read spreadsheets, send emails, create calendar events, and interact with the services you already use on the farm.

FarmClaw integrations are powered by Composio, a platform that provides secure, authenticated access to 800+ third-party APIs through a single connection. Composio runs as a remote MCP server alongside your built-in farm tools.

How it works

When you enable integrations, FarmClaw:

1. Creates a Composio entity for your account (no separate Composio sign-up required).
2. Provisions a remote MCP server that your assistant can call.
3. Adds the server to your workspace configuration alongside your existing MCP servers.

From that point, your assistant has access to the actions exposed by your selected toolkits. You authorize each service individually through a standard OAuth flow -- the same "Sign in with Google" experience you use elsewhere.

Key concepts

Toolkits

A toolkit is a bundle of related actions for a single service. For example, the Google Sheets toolkit includes actions for reading spreadsheets, writing data, and creating new sheets. You select which toolkits your assistant has access to -- only selected toolkits are available.

FarmClaw curates a set of toolkits with descriptions tailored to farming use cases. See Available Toolkits for the full list.

Connections

A connection is an authorized link between your assistant and a specific service. When you connect Google Sheets, for example, you go through Google's OAuth consent screen and grant your assistant permission to access your spreadsheets. The connection is managed by Composio -- your Google credentials are never stored on FarmClaw servers.

See Connecting Services for the full OAuth walkthrough.

Remote MCP servers

Composio runs as a remote MCP server over Server-Sent Events (SSE). Your assistant connects to Composio's hosted endpoint, which routes requests to the appropriate service API.

Composio runs entirely on Composio's infrastructure.

Composio integration is included in your FarmClaw subscription. The Composio API key is platform-level -- you do not need your own Composio account or API key.

What can your assistant do with integrations?

Here are some real examples of what farmers can ask once integrations are connected:

| Service | Example prompt | What happens |
|---|---|---|
| Google Sheets | "Add today's scale tickets to my harvest tracker spreadsheet" | Reads your ticket data, writes rows to your Google Sheet |
| Google Sheets | "Pull my input costs from the planning sheet" | Reads specific cells/ranges from a spreadsheet |
| Gmail | "Email the grain contract summary to my wife" | Composes and sends an email with delivery data |
| Google Calendar | "Schedule spraying for next Tuesday at 6 AM" | Creates a calendar event with details |
| Google Calendar | "What's on the farm calendar this week?" | Lists upcoming events |
| Slack | "Post today's weather summary to the farm-updates channel" | Sends a message to a Slack channel |
| Notion | "Save this field scouting report to Notion" | Creates a page in your Notion workspace |

Integrations complement your existing skills. Your assistant can combine data from skills (grain bids, weather, scale tickets) with integration actions (write to spreadsheet, send email, create calendar event) in a single response.

Getting started

1. Go to Settings > Integrations in your FarmClaw dashboard.
2. Toggle the Composio switch to enable the integration.
3. Select the toolkits you want your assistant to access.
4. Click Save Toolkits to apply changes.
5. Connect each service by clicking Connect and completing the OAuth flow.

For detailed setup instructions, see Setup Guide.

Privacy and security

- Your service credentials (Google tokens, Slack tokens, etc.) are encrypted with AES-256-GCM and managed by Composio. They are never stored on FarmClaw servers.
- Composio acts as a secure proxy -- your assistant accesses services through authorized API calls only.
- Disconnecting a service immediately revokes all access and removes stored tokens.
- You control exactly which toolkits your assistant can use. Disabling a toolkit removes the assistant's ability to call those actions.

See Security and Privacy for full details.

What's next

- Setup Guide -- Step-by-step walkthrough of enabling Composio and connecting services
- Available Toolkits -- Full list of curated toolkits with farming-specific descriptions
- Connecting Services -- OAuth flow, popup handling, and troubleshooting
- Security and Privacy -- How credentials are stored, what data flows where, and how to revoke access

title: Security and Privacy

headings.title: When your assistant uses an integration
Can FarmClaw employees access my Google Sheets / emails / calendar?
What happens to my data if I cancel my FarmClaw subscription?
Can I use integrations without giving FarmClaw access to my email?

body:
Security and Privacy

This page explains how FarmClaw's Composio integration handles your credentials, what data flows between systems, and what controls you have over access.

Architecture overview

Three systems are involved in the integrations flow:

| System | What it stores | What it does |
|---|---|---|
| FarmClaw | Composio entity ID, MCP server URL, toolkit selections, cached connection status | Manages configuration, serves the dashboard UI |
| Composio | OAuth tokens (access + refresh), connection metadata | Handles OAuth flows, stores credentials, proxies API calls, refreshes tokens |
| Service (Google, etc.) | Your account data | Processes API requests from Composio on your behalf |

What FarmClaw does NOT store

FarmClaw never stores:

- Your service passwords
- OAuth access tokens
- OAuth refresh tokens
- Service API keys
- Any credentials for Google, Slack, Notion, or other connected services

The only credential-related data FarmClaw stores is the connection status (connected/disconnected/expired) and the account identifier (e.g., the email address associated with the connection). These are cached from Composio for display in the dashboard.

Credential encryption

Composio encrypts all stored credentials using AES-256-GCM encryption at rest. This is the same encryption standard used by banks and government systems. Credentials are decrypted only at the moment they are needed to make an API call on your behalf.

AES-256-GCM is an authenticated encryption algorithm that provides both confidentiality and integrity. It uses a 256-bit key, making brute-force attacks computationally infeasible. The GCM mode adds authentication, ensuring encrypted data cannot be tampered with.

Data flow

When your assistant uses an integration

Here is the complete data flow when your assistant performs an integration action (e.g., writing to a Google Sheet):

1. Your assistant decides it needs to call a Google Sheets action based on your Discord message.
2. The assistant sends a tool call request to the Composio MCP server via SSE (Server-Sent Events).
3. Composio receives the request, identifies your entity, and retrieves your stored OAuth token.
4. Composio makes the API call to Google Sheets using your token.
5. Google Sheets processes the request and returns the result.
6. Composio forwards the result back to your assistant via SSE.
7. Your assistant uses the result to compose its response in Discord.

What data Composio can see

Composio acts as a proxy. When processing your requests, Composio can see:

- The action being called (e.g., "read spreadsheet")
- The parameters of the action (e.g., spreadsheet ID, cell range)
- The response from the service (e.g., cell values)

Composio does not:

- Store the content of your requests or responses beyond what is needed for the immediate API call
- Share your data with other Composio users
- Use your data for training or analytics
- Access your connected services outside of explicit tool calls from your assistant

What FarmClaw can see

FarmClaw can see:

- That you have Composio enabled
- Which toolkits you have selected
- Which services are connected (status only, not tokens)
- Standard API usage metrics (request counts for billing)

FarmClaw cannot see:

- The content of integration requests or responses (these flow directly between your assistant and Composio)
- Your OAuth tokens or service credentials
- Your service account data (spreadsheet contents, emails, calendar events, etc.)

Access controls

You control what is enabled

You have several layers of control over what your assistant can access through integrations:

| Control | What it does | How to change |
|---|---|---|
| Composio toggle | Enables/disables all integrations | Settings > Integrations > toggle switch |
| Toolkit selection | Controls which services are available | Settings > Integrations > Toolkits > Save |
| Service connection | Authorizes/revokes access to specific services | Settings > Integrations > Connect/Disconnect |
| OAuth consent | Grants specific permission scopes | During the OAuth flow (Google consent screen, etc.) |

Principle of least privilege

Each layer is independent:

- Disabling Composio removes all integration access, regardless of toolkit or connection state.
- Deselecting a toolkit removes that service's actions even if the connection is active.
- Disconnecting a service revokes access even if the toolkit is selected.
- You can limit OAuth scopes during the consent step (though this may prevent some actions from working).

Visit the Connected Accounts section periodically to review which services are connected and disconnect any you no longer need. Less access surface means less risk.

Disconnection and revocation

Disconnecting from FarmClaw

When you click Disconnect in the FarmClaw dashboard:

1. FarmClaw calls Composio's API to revoke the connection.
2. Composio deletes the stored OAuth tokens.
3. The connection status updates to "Disconnected" in FarmClaw.
4. Your assistant immediately loses access to that service.

Revoking from the service side

You can also revoke access directly from the service provider (e.g., Google Account > Security > Third-party apps). When you do:

1. The service invalidates the OAuth tokens.
2. Composio's next attempt to use the token will fail.
3. The connection status will show as "Expired" after a refresh.
4. Your assistant will receive an authorization error when trying to use the service.

Both methods are equally effective. Disconnecting from FarmClaw is the recommended approach because it provides immediate UI feedback.

Transport security

FarmClaw to Composio

All communication between FarmClaw and Composio occurs over HTTPS (TLS 1.2+). The MCP server connection uses Server-Sent Events (SSE) over HTTPS.

Composio to services

Composio communicates with service APIs over HTTPS. Composio does not support unencrypted connections to service providers.

Your browser to FarmClaw

The FarmClaw dashboard is served over HTTPS. OAuth callbacks are also HTTPS-only.

Incident response

If Composio is compromised

In the unlikely event of a Composio security incident:

1. Immediately disconnect all services from the FarmClaw dashboard.
2. Revoke FarmClaw's access from each service provider's security settings.
3. Disable the Composio integration toggle.
4. Change passwords on your connected service accounts.

If FarmClaw is compromised

A FarmClaw compromise would not expose your service credentials because FarmClaw does not store them. However, an attacker with access to your FarmClaw account could:

- See which services are connected (status only)
- Enable/disable toolkits
- Trigger new OAuth flows (but would need your service password to complete them)

To mitigate: use a strong password and magic link authentication for your FarmClaw account.

Frequently asked questions

Can FarmClaw employees access my Google Sheets / emails / calendar?

No. FarmClaw does not store your service credentials and does not have access to your connected service accounts. Only your AI assistant can make API calls through Composio using your authorized tokens.

What happens to my data if I cancel my FarmClaw subscription?

When your subscription ends and your workspace is closed:

- Your Composio entity and all stored tokens are deleted.
- All service connections are revoked.
- No residual access to your services remains.

Can I use integrations without giving FarmClaw access to my email?

Yes. Each toolkit is independent. You can enable Google Sheets and Google Calendar without enabling Gmail. You choose exactly which services to connect.

Is Composio SOC 2 / GDPR compliant?

Composio maintains enterprise security practices. Check composio.dev/security for their latest compliance certifications and security documentation.

What's next

- Integrations Overview -- How integrations fit into your FarmClaw workspace
- Connecting Services -- OAuth flow and connection management
- Troubleshooting -- Common issues and solutions

title: Setup Guide

headings.title: Assistant says it cannot access a service

body:
Setup Guide

This guide walks you through enabling Composio integrations, selecting toolkits, and connecting your first third-party service.

Prerequisites

Before you can enable integrations, you need:

- An active FarmClaw subscription
- An active workspace (your AI assistant must already be set up)
- Access to the FarmClaw dashboard

If your workspace is not yet set up, complete the onboarding wizard first.

Step 1: Enable Composio

1. Navigate to Settings > Integrations in your FarmClaw dashboard.
2. You will see the Composio integration card at the top of the page.
3. Toggle the switch to enable the integration.

When you enable Composio, FarmClaw performs several setup steps behind the scenes:

- Creates a Composio entity for your account
- Provisions a remote MCP server endpoint
- Stores the server configuration in your database
- Adds the Composio MCP server to your workspace configuration

Enabling Composio takes just a few seconds. Your assistant gains access to integration tools immediately.

This is a one-time setup. Once enabled, adding or removing toolkits and connecting services is instant.

Step 2: Select toolkits

After enabling Composio, the Toolkits section appears below the integration card.

1. Browse the available toolkits using the category tabs: Recommended, Productivity, Communication, Data, or All.
2. Click on a toolkit card to select or deselect it.
3. Selected toolkits show a green checkmark and highlighted border.
4. Click Save Toolkits when you have made your selections.

Only the actions from your selected toolkits will be available to your assistant.

The Recommended tab shows the toolkits most useful for farm operations: Google Sheets, Google Calendar, Gmail, and Slack. These cover the most common integration use cases -- spreadsheet tracking, scheduling, email, and notifications.

Changing toolkit selections

You can add or remove toolkits at any time:

1. Select or deselect toolkits as needed.
2. The Save Toolkits button becomes active when your selections differ from the saved configuration.
3. Click Save Toolkits to apply changes.

Removing a toolkit does not disconnect the service. If you have Google Sheets connected and you deselect the Google Sheets toolkit, the connection remains intact but the assistant loses the ability to call Google Sheets actions. Re-selecting the toolkit restores access without needing to reconnect.

Step 3: Connect a service

After selecting toolkits, the Connected Accounts section shows all selected toolkits that require authentication.

1. Find the service you want to connect (e.g., Google Sheets).
2. Click the Connect button next to it.
3. A confirmation dialog appears showing:
- The service name
- The specific actions your assistant will have access to
- A privacy note about how credentials are handled
4. Click Authorize [Service Name] to proceed.

OAuth popup flow

After clicking Authorize:

1. A popup window opens with the service's login/consent page (e.g., Google's "Choose an account" screen).
2. Sign in to your account if prompted.
3. Review the permissions requested and click Allow or Grant Access.
4. The popup displays a "Connected" message and closes automatically after 2 seconds.
5. The parent page updates to show the service as connected with a green "Connected" badge.
6. A success toast notification appears: "Service connected successfully".

The OAuth flow uses a popup window. If your browser blocks the popup, FarmClaw falls back to a full-page redirect. To avoid interruption, allow popups for your FarmClaw dashboard URL.

Connection status indicators

After connecting, each service shows a status badge:

| Badge | Meaning |
|---|---|
| Connected (green) | Active connection. Your assistant can use this service. |
| Expired (amber) | Token has expired. Click Connect to re-authorize. |
| Disconnected (gray) | Not connected. Click Connect to start the OAuth flow. |
| Pending (blue) | OAuth flow in progress. |

Step 4: Test the integration

Once a service is connected, test it by sending a message to your assistant in Discord:

Google Sheets test

Gmail test

Google Calendar test

If the assistant responds with data from the service, your integration is working correctly.

Disabling Composio

To disable the entire Composio integration:

1. Navigate to Settings > Integrations.
2. Toggle the Composio switch to off.
3. The Composio MCP server is removed from your workspace configuration.
4. Your assistant loses access to all integration actions.

Disabling Composio does not disconnect your service accounts. If you re-enable Composio later, your existing connections will still be active. To fully disconnect, remove each service connection before disabling.

Troubleshooting

"Failed to enable integration"

This usually means the Composio API is temporarily unavailable. Wait a minute and try again. If the issue persists, check the Troubleshooting page.

OAuth popup does not appear

Your browser may be blocking popups. Check your browser's popup blocker settings and allow popups for your FarmClaw dashboard domain.

Service shows "Expired"

OAuth tokens expire periodically. Click Connect next to the expired service to re-authorize. The flow is the same as the initial connection.

Assistant says it cannot access a service

Verify that:

1. Composio is enabled (toggle is on).
2. The toolkit for that service is selected and saved.
3. The service is connected (green "Connected" badge).
4. You have saved your toolkit selections since the last change.

If all four conditions are met and the assistant still cannot access the service, click Refresh in the Connected Accounts section to sync the latest connection status from Composio.

What's next

- Available Toolkits -- Full list of services with farming-specific descriptions
- Connecting Services -- Deep dive into the OAuth flow and connection management
- Security and Privacy -- How credentials are protected

title: Available Toolkits
body:
Available Toolkits

FarmClaw curates a set of Composio toolkits with descriptions and recommendations tailored to Upper Midwest row crop farming. Each toolkit bundles the most useful actions for a single service.

Recommended toolkits

These four toolkits cover the most common integration use cases for farmers. They are highlighted in the Recommended tab when selecting toolkits.

Google Sheets

| | |
|---|---|
| Category | Recommended |
| Requires auth | Yes (Google account) |
| Actions | Read Spreadsheet, Write Spreadsheet, Create Spreadsheet |

What it does: Read and write Google Sheets spreadsheets directly from Discord.

Farm use cases:

- Track harvest deliveries in a shared spreadsheet alongside scale ticket data
- Log daily field observations and input applications
- Pull cost-per-acre numbers from a planning spreadsheet
- Build season summary reports that update automatically

Example prompts:

Google Sheets works great alongside the Scale Tickets skill. Ask your assistant to "export this week's tickets to my harvest tracker spreadsheet" and it will read your ticket data and write to Google Sheets in one step.

Google Calendar

| | |
|---|---|
| Category | Recommended |
| Requires auth | Yes (Google account) |
| Actions | Create Event, List Events, Update Event |

What it does: Manage Google Calendar events -- create, read, and update scheduling entries.

Farm use cases:

- Schedule planting and spraying windows based on weather forecasts
- Block time for equipment maintenance and field work
- Track delivery appointments at elevators
- Set reminders for crop insurance deadlines

Example prompts:

Ask your assistant to check the forecast and schedule accordingly: "Find the next 3-day window with no rain and schedule bean spraying."

Gmail

| | |
|---|---|
| Category | Recommended |
| Requires auth | Yes (Google account) |
| Actions | Send Email, List Emails, Read Email |

What it does: Send and read Gmail messages through your assistant.

Farm use cases:

- Forward grain contract summaries to partners or family
- Email daily delivery reports to your accountant
- Share weather alerts with crew members
- Send equipment breakdown notifications

Example prompts:

When your assistant sends an email through Gmail, it sends from your actual Gmail account. Double-check recipients and content before confirming sends, especially for the first few uses.

Slack

| | |
|---|---|
| Category | Communication |
| Requires auth | Yes (Slack workspace) |
| Actions | Send Message, List Channels |

What it does: Post messages to Slack channels from your assistant.

Farm use cases:

- Cross-post weather alerts and market reports from Discord to Slack
- Notify a shared Slack channel when a large delivery is logged
- Share daily grain bid summaries with a team channel

Example prompts:

Productivity toolkits

Notion

| | |
|---|---|
| Category | Productivity |
| Requires auth | Yes (Notion account) |
| Actions | Create Page, Search, Update Page |

What it does: Create and search Notion pages from your assistant.

Farm use cases:

- Build a searchable knowledge base of field notes and SOPs
- Document crop plans and seasonal strategies
- Store equipment maintenance logs
- Keep running notes on field performance year over year

Example prompts:

Google Drive

| | |
|---|---|
| Category | Productivity |
| Requires auth | Yes (Google account) |
| Actions | List Files, Upload File, Download File |

What it does: Access and organize files in Google Drive.

Farm use cases:

- Store and retrieve field maps, soil test results, and aerial imagery
- Access grain contracts and insurance documents
- Organize photos by field and season

Example prompts:

Todoist

| | |
|---|---|
| Category | Productivity |
| Requires auth | Yes (Todoist account) |
| Actions | Create Task, List Tasks, Complete Task |

What it does: Manage tasks and to-do lists.

Farm use cases:

- Track maintenance tasks for equipment
- Create to-do lists for fieldwork preparation
- Manage delivery schedules and logistics

Example prompts:

Data toolkits

Airtable

| | |
|---|---|
| Category | Data |
| Requires auth | Yes (Airtable account) |
| Actions | Create Record, List Records, Update Record |

What it does: Read and write records in Airtable bases.

Farm use cases:

- Manage equipment inventory with structured records
- Track seed lot information across fields and years
- Log chemical and fertilizer applications with rates and dates
- Build custom databases for any tracking need

Example prompts:

Toolkit selection tips

Start small

Enable only the toolkits you will actively use. Each toolkit adds actions to your assistant's tool list, and a smaller tool list means faster, more focused responses. You can always add more toolkits later.

Recommended first

The four recommended toolkits (Google Sheets, Google Calendar, Gmail, Slack) cover 90% of integration use cases for most farms. Start with these and expand as needed.

Combine with skills

The most powerful workflows combine FarmClaw skills with integration actions:

| Skill | + Integration | Result |
|---|---|---|
| Scale Tickets | Google Sheets | Auto-export delivery data to spreadsheets |
| Weather | Google Calendar | Schedule field work around forecast windows |
| Grain Bids | Gmail | Email daily bid reports to partners |
| Weather | Slack | Cross-post alerts to team channels |

What's next

- Setup Guide -- Enable Composio and select your toolkits
- Connecting Services -- Authorize your accounts
- Security and Privacy -- How your credentials are protected

title: Filesystem Server
body:
Filesystem Server

The Filesystem MCP server gives your AI assistant the ability to read, write, and manage files. This is useful for saving reports, keeping notes, storing exported data, and working with files that persist across conversations.

Overview

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Package | @modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem |
| Command | npx |
| Arguments | -y @modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem |
| Scope | Workspace (available to all agents) |

The Filesystem server is a custom MCP server you can add to your workspace if you need file read/write capabilities. It is not required for any built-in farm tools -- scale tickets, grain bids, weather, and memory all work without it.

What it enables

With the Filesystem server active, your assistant can:

- Read files -- Open and read the contents of any file in its workspace directory.
- Write files -- Create new files or overwrite existing ones. Useful for saving reports, exporting data, or generating documents.
- List directory contents -- Browse what files and folders exist in the workspace.
- Create directories -- Organize files into folders as needed.
- Move and rename files -- Restructure workspace content without manual intervention.

Use cases

Saving reports

Ask your assistant to generate a summary and save it to a file:

> "Summarize this week's grain bids across all elevators and save the report to reports/weekly-bids-2025-10-13.md."

The assistant will compile the data and write it to the specified path.

Exporting scale ticket data

If you want a local copy of your scale ticket records:

> "Export all corn deliveries from October as a CSV file."

The assistant writes the CSV to the workspace where it persists between conversations.

Keeping operational notes

Your assistant can maintain running notes, checklists, or logs:

> "Add a note to field-notes.md that the south quarter of section 12 had standing water after last night's rain."

Files saved by the Filesystem server persist between conversations and are available any time you interact with your assistant.

Adding the Filesystem server

1. Go to Settings > MCP Servers.
2. Click Add MCP Server.
3. Select From Preset.
4. Click Filesystem from the preset list.
5. Review the pre-filled fields and click Add Server.

The server is available to your assistant after saving.

Enabling and disabling

You can toggle the Filesystem server on or off at any time using the switch on its card in Settings > MCP Servers. Disabling the server removes it from your assistant's active tools. Your files are not deleted when the server is disabled.

What's next

- MCP Servers Overview -- Learn about custom MCP server configuration

title: MCP Servers
body:
MCP Servers

Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers let you extend your AI assistant with custom tools and data access beyond the built-in farm tools. MCP servers are for advanced or custom tooling -- the core farm capabilities (scale tickets, grain bids, weather, and memory) are handled natively by the FarmClaw tenant runtime and do not require any MCP configuration.

What is MCP?

MCP is an open protocol that lets AI assistants connect to external tool servers using a standardized interface. Instead of building every tool directly into the AI model, MCP servers expose capabilities that the assistant can call on demand. This makes it possible to integrate specialized or third-party tools without modifying the core assistant.

When your assistant needs to use a custom tool provided by an MCP server, it sends a request to that server. The server processes the request and returns the result, which the assistant then uses to answer your question.

Scale tickets, grain bids, weather forecasts, and memory are all built into the FarmClaw platform. They work out of the box with no MCP server setup. MCP servers are only needed if you want to add custom or third-party tools.

When to use MCP servers

MCP servers are useful when you need your assistant to access tools or data sources that are not covered by the built-in farm skills. Examples include:

- A custom internal API for your farm management software
- A specialized data source or database
- Third-party tool servers published by the MCP community
- Filesystem access for saving and reading custom files

Adding a custom MCP server

MCP servers are managed through the FarmClaw dashboard.

1. Go to Settings > MCP Servers.
2. Click Add MCP Server.
3. Fill in the required fields and click Add Server.

You can enable, disable, edit, or delete servers at any time.

When adding a custom server, you will need to provide:

| Field | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| serverkey | Unique lowercase identifier (letters, numbers, hyphens) | my-custom-server |
| displayname | Human-readable label shown in the dashboard | My Custom Server |
| command | The executable to run | npx |
| args | Arguments passed to the command (space-separated) | -y @my-org/my-mcp-server /data |
| envvars | Optional environment variables for the server process | {"APIKEY": "..."} |
| scope | instance (all agents) or agent (specific agent only) | instance |
| enabled | Whether the server is active | true |

The serverkey cannot be changed after creation. If you need a different key, delete the server and create a new one.

Scoping

MCP servers are defined at the workspace level by default, meaning every agent in a multi-agent setup can use them. You can also scope a server to a specific agent if you want to restrict access.

Each server is identified by a unique serverkey. The key is used internally to reference the server in the assistant's configuration.

What's next

- Filesystem Server -- Example: read and write files in a workspace directory
- Extensions -- Add more capabilities through the extension marketplace

title: Custom Skills
body:
Custom Skills

Beyond the three built-in skills, FarmClaw lets you create your own custom skills using the Skill Forge. Custom skills extend your assistant with new capabilities tailored to your specific operation.

What are custom skills?

A custom skill is a set of instructions written in Markdown (SKILL.md format) that tells your assistant how to handle a specific type of request. You can create up to 20 active custom skills per workspace, each up to 50KB in size.

Creating a skill

Using AI generation

The fastest way to create a custom skill is with AI generation:

1. Go to Settings > Skills > Forge
2. Click Create Skill
3. Select Generate with AI
4. Describe what you want the skill to do in plain English (10--5,000 characters)
5. Optionally toggle Include farm context to auto-include your farm name, location, crops, acres, and elevators
6. FarmClaw streams a complete SKILL.md in real time
7. Review the generated skill, make any adjustments, and save

The more detail you provide, the better the generated skill. Include examples of questions the skill should handle, what data it should use, and how responses should be formatted.

Writing manually

You can also write a skill from scratch using the built-in Markdown editor with formatting toolbar (bold, italic, headings, code blocks, lists) and live preview.

Using templates

FarmClaw provides eight built-in templates for common farm skill patterns:

| Template | Category |
|---|---|
| Crop Scouting Report | Crop Management |
| Harvest Yield Tracker | Crop Management |
| Field Notes | Crop Management |
| Input Cost Calculator | Financial |
| Market Alert Watcher | Financial |
| Equipment Maintenance Log | Equipment |
| Livestock Feed Ration | Livestock |
| FSA/Crop Insurance Reporter | Compliance |

Select a template when creating a new skill to get a working starting point you can customize.

SKILL.md format

Custom skills use a Markdown-based format with YAML frontmatter:

The name field must start with custom- and match the skill's slug.

Version history

Every time you save changes to a custom skill, FarmClaw creates a new version. From the version history panel you can:

- View all versions -- See each version with its timestamp and change summary
- Compare -- The current version is highlighted for easy identification
- Roll back -- Click the rollback button on any previous version to restore it. Rollbacks create a new version so you never lose history.

Testing

Test your skill before deploying it to your live assistant:

1. Open the skill in the Forge editor
2. Switch to the Test tab
3. Enter a sample message that should trigger the skill
4. Watch the assistant's streaming response in real time
5. Continue the conversation with follow-up messages to iterate

The test panel shows a full chat interface. Draft skills run in preview mode; active skills test against your live assistant.

Skill lifecycle

| Status | Meaning |
|---|---|
| Draft | Created but not assigned to agents. Testing available. |
| Active | Deployed and available for agent assignment. |
| Archived | Soft-deleted. Cannot be updated. Automatically removed from all agents. |

Reference files

You can attach up to 10 reference files (max 50KB each) to a custom skill to give your assistant additional context -- product manuals, lookup tables, conversion charts, or farm-specific documentation.

What's next

- Skills Overview -- Learn about the built-in skills
- Knowledge Files -- Upload documents for your assistant to reference
- AI Agents -- Assign skills to specific agents

title: Grain Bids
body:
Grain Bids

The Grain Bids skill scrapes your configured elevator websites using a browser tool to extract currently posted cash grain bid information. It fetches live data every time you ask -- no cached or stale prices.

How it works

When you ask about grain prices, the assistant:

1. Opens each configured elevator's cash bid webpage in a headless browser.
2. Takes an ARIA snapshot of the page to understand its structure.
3. Locates the cash bid table and extracts rows matching your configured crops.
4. If the page requires interaction (e.g., selecting a location dropdown), the assistant interacts with the page elements as needed.
5. Presents the results in a clean, scannable format with timestamps.

The assistant never uses remembered or cached prices from previous conversations. Every bid request triggers a live scrape of the elevator website. This means your data is always as current as the elevator has posted.

What it fetches

For each elevator, the assistant extracts the following fields when available:

| Field | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Commodity | The crop name | Corn, Soybeans, Spring Wheat |
| Bid price | Cash price per bushel | $4.52 |
| Basis | Cents +/- relative to futures contract | -35 |
| Delivery period | Spot, deferred, or forward months | Spot, Dec 2025 |
| Futures month | The reference futures contract month | Mar 26, May 26 |

Output format

Bids are displayed in a clean tabular format organized by commodity:

When you ask about a single elevator, the assistant shows all available commodities and delivery periods for that location.

Comparison mode

When you ask to compare elevators, the assistant:

1. Fetches bids from all configured elevators.
2. Shows a side-by-side comparison sorted by best price per commodity.
3. Highlights the best bid for each commodity so you can see where to get the most per bushel at a glance.

Configuration

The Grain Bids skill reads your elevator list from your farm profile. Each elevator entry includes:

| Setting | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Name | Display name of the elevator | CHS Fargo |
| URL | The page where cash bids are posted | https://www.chsinc.com/... |
| Crops | Which commodities to track at this elevator | Corn, Soybeans |

You configure elevators during onboarding and can update them at any time in Settings > Farm Profile > Elevators. FarmClaw provides presets for major elevator companies with state-specific defaults:

- CHS
- ADM
- Cargill
- AGP
- Columbia Grain
- Gavilon

The presets cover major companies, but you can add any elevator that posts cash bids on a public webpage. Just provide the name, the URL of their bid page, and which crops to track.

Understanding basis

Basis is the difference between the local cash price and the nearby futures contract price, expressed in cents per bushel.

- Negative basis (e.g., -35) means the cash price is 35 cents under the futures price. This is typical for inland locations.
- Positive basis (e.g., +10) means the cash price is above futures. This is less common and usually indicates strong local demand.
- Narrowing basis (e.g., -35 moving to -25) means the local cash price is improving relative to futures.

The assistant always reports basis alongside the cash bid so you can evaluate both the absolute price and the local premium or discount.

Error handling

Not every scrape succeeds every time. The assistant handles failures gracefully:

| Situation | What the assistant does |
|---|---|
| Elevator website is unreachable | Reports which elevator failed, shows results from others |
| Page structure changed | Tells you the site may have been updated, suggests checking directly |
| Bids not found for a crop | Indicates no bids were found for that commodity at that elevator |
| All elevators fail | Reports the issue and suggests trying again later |

The assistant always includes a timestamp showing when the data was fetched.

Most elevators update their posted cash bids in the morning. If you check in the evening, the bids shown may be from that morning's posting. The assistant will note the time of the data.

Automated daily bids

You can set up a Daily Grain Bids cron job to have the assistant automatically post bids every weekday morning. This runs Monday through Friday at 9:00 AM in your local timezone.

To enable it:

1. Go to Settings > Agents.
2. Select the agent you want to run the job (typically your Grain Desk or General Advisor).
3. Toggle on Daily Grain Bids under Scheduled Jobs.
4. Save changes.

See Skills & Scheduling for more on cron job configuration.

Example prompts

Here are some ways to ask about grain bids in Discord:

The last example will generate an .xlsx file with elevator, crop, bid, and basis columns and send it as a Discord attachment.

File exports

The assistant can export bid data as files:

| Format | Use case | How to ask |
|---|---|---|
| Excel (.xlsx) | Bid summaries, elevator comparisons | "Put those bids in a spreadsheet" |
| CSV | Quick tabular export | "Export bids as CSV" |
| PNG chart | Basis history or comparison charts | "Chart the basis" |
| PDF | Formatted grain marketing summary | "Send me a bid report as PDF" |

Files are sent as Discord message attachments (25 MB limit).

title: Skills
body:
Skills

Skills define what your AI assistant can actually do. Each skill provides a set of instructions, tools, and workflows that enable your assistant to perform specific farm tasks -- from fetching grain bids off elevator websites to reading scale tickets from a photo.

Built-in skills

Every FarmClaw workspace ships with three built-in skills. These cover the core tasks that Upper Midwest row crop farmers use daily.

| Skill | What it does | Key capability |
|---|---|---|
| Grain Bids | Scrapes elevator websites for cash grain prices | Real-time bid comparison across all your elevators |
| Weather Forecasts | Agriculture-focused weather from the National Weather Service | Spray window analysis, GDD tracking, morning briefings |
| Scale Tickets | Logs grain deliveries via photo, voice, or guided entry | OCR from ticket photos, natural language parsing, export to spreadsheet |

The $50/month subscription includes all built-in skills. There is no tiered feature gating. The only variable cost is metered API usage based on how often you interact with your assistant.

How skills work

Skills define the tools and instructions available to your assistant. Each skill contains:

- Instructions -- Detailed guidance that tells the assistant how to perform the task, what data to look for, and how to format the output.
- Tools -- Access to browser automation, APIs, databases, or file creation capabilities needed to complete the task.
- Configuration -- Farm-specific settings like your elevator list, weather coordinates, or which scale ticket fields to display.

When you ask your assistant a question, it determines which skill applies and follows the skill's instructions to produce a response. For example, asking "What's corn at?" triggers the Grain Bids skill, which navigates to your elevator websites and extracts the current posted prices.

Enabling and disabling skills

You can toggle skills on or off from the FarmClaw dashboard.

1. Go to Settings > Skills.
2. Toggle the switch next to each skill to enable or disable it.
3. Click Save Changes.

When you save skill settings, FarmClaw applies the changes to your assistant. Updates take effect on the next message.

Disabling a skill removes it from your assistant's configuration entirely. The assistant will not attempt tasks related to a disabled skill, even if asked directly.

Skills and agents

If you run a multi-agent setup, each agent gets its own subset of the globally-enabled skills. This lets you create focused specialists:

- A Grain Desk agent with only the Grain Bids skill
- A Weather Watch agent with only the Weather Forecasts skill
- A Ticket Clerk agent with only the Scale Tickets skill
- A General Advisor that has all skills for catch-all questions

A skill must be enabled globally before it can be assigned to any agent. Disabling a skill globally removes it from all agents.

| Configuration level | Where to change it | What it controls |
|---|---|---|
| Global skill toggle | Settings > Skills | Whether the skill is available at all |
| Per-agent assignment | Settings > Agents > [Agent] | Which agents can use the skill |

Skill configuration

Each skill has its own configuration that comes from your farm profile and onboarding data:

- Grain Bids reads your elevator list (name, URL, crops to track) from your farm setup.
- Weather Forecasts reads your latitude, longitude, location name, and timezone.
- Scale Tickets reads your crop list for bushel weight conversions and your visible/required field preferences.

To update these settings, edit the relevant section in your farm profile or skill-specific settings page. See each skill's documentation for details.

What's next

- Grain Bids -- Real-time cash grain prices from your elevators
- Weather Forecasts -- NWS-powered forecasts, spray windows, and GDD
- Scale Tickets -- Three ways to log grain deliveries
- Skills & Scheduling -- Assign skills to specific agents and set up cron jobs

title: Scale Tickets
body:
Scale Tickets

The Scale Tickets skill lets you log, track, query, and export grain delivery records directly from Discord. It supports three entry methods -- snap a photo of the ticket, dictate the details by voice or text, or walk through a guided entry flow. All data is stored securely in your farm's cloud database.

How it works

Scale ticket data is stored securely in your farm's cloud database as part of the FarmClaw platform. When you enable the Scale Tickets skill, your assistant can immediately start recording and querying delivery records. No additional setup is required.

Your scale ticket database is provisioned automatically when you enable the Scale Tickets skill. No setup required on your end.

Each ticket record can include:

| Field | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Date | Delivery date | 10/15/2025 |
| Elevator | Where the grain was delivered | CHS Fargo |
| Crop | Commodity type | Corn |
| Gross weight | Weight before unloading (lbs) | 82,340 |
| Tare weight | Truck weight after unloading (lbs) | 34,120 |
| Net weight | Grain weight (gross - tare) (lbs) | 48,220 |
| Moisture % | Moisture reading at delivery | 15.2% |
| Test weight | Weight per bushel (lb/bu) | 56.4 |
| Dockage % | Percentage docked for foreign material | 0.5% |
| Price per bushel | Cash price received | $4.52 |
| Grade | Grain grade designation | 2 Yellow |
| Truck number | Which truck made the delivery | Truck 3 |
| Load number | Sequential load identifier | 47 |
| Field name | Which field the grain came from | North 80 |
| Notes | Any additional notes | Slight dock for moisture |

Three entry methods

1. Photo OCR

Post a photo of your paper scale ticket in the Discord channel. The assistant uses AI vision to read the ticket and extract all visible fields.

How it works:

1. Take a photo of the scale ticket with your phone.
2. Post the image in Discord.
3. The assistant reads the image and presents the extracted data for confirmation.
4. Review the summary. If anything is wrong, tell the assistant what to change.
5. Reply "save" to record the ticket.

Lay the ticket flat on a dark surface. Make sure the text is in focus and well-lit. The assistant can read most printed and handwritten tickets, but legibility matters. If a field is unclear, the assistant will tell you which ones it could not read and ask you to fill in the gaps.

2. Voice / unstructured text

Type or dictate the ticket details naturally. The assistant parses your message, extracts the fields, and confirms before saving. This is the fastest method when you are in the truck and want to log a load quickly.

Example inputs:

The assistant uses these parsing rules to interpret your message:

| Pattern | Interpretation |
|---|---|
| "k" or "K" after a number | Thousands (48k = 48,000 lbs) |
| "beans" | Soybeans |
| "durum" | Durum Wheat |
| "spring wheat" or "HRS" | Spring Wheat |
| Numbers 40,000--90,000 | Likely net weight in pounds |
| Numbers 400--1,800 | Likely bushels |
| Numbers 10--25 with decimal | Likely moisture percentage |
| Numbers 45--65 with decimal | Likely test weight (lb/bu) |

If no date is mentioned, today's date is used. The assistant converts between pounds and bushels automatically using the standard bushel weights table.

3. Multi-step structured entry

Say "new ticket", "log a ticket", or "add scale ticket" to start a guided entry flow. The assistant walks you through four groups of fields, one at a time.

Group 1: Basics

- Date -- Defaults to today if not specified
- Elevator -- Suggests your configured elevators
- Crop -- Suggests your configured crops

Group 2: Weights

- Gross weight -- Weight before unloading
- Tare weight -- Truck weight after unloading
- Net weight -- Auto-calculated (gross minus tare). You can also provide net weight directly.
- The assistant asks "pounds or bushels?" if the unit is unclear.

Group 3: Quality (skippable -- say "skip" or "no quality data")

- Moisture %
- Test weight (lb/bu)
- Dockage %

Group 4: Details (skippable)

- Price per bushel
- Grade
- Truck number
- Load number
- Field name
- Notes

After all groups, the assistant presents the complete ticket for confirmation before saving.

Your admin can configure which fields are visible and which are required using the visiblefields and requiredfields settings. If a field is not in your visible fields list, the assistant will not ask for it during structured entry -- but will still accept it if provided via photo OCR or voice.

Bushel weight table

The assistant uses these standard bushel weights for all pound-to-bushel conversions:

| Crop | Lbs per bushel |
|---|---|
| Corn | 56 |
| Soybeans | 60 |
| Spring Wheat | 60 |
| Winter Wheat | 60 |
| Durum | 60 |
| Barley | 48 |
| Sunflowers | 24 |
| Canola | 50 |
| Dry Beans | 60 |
| Flax | 56 |

When displaying totals, the assistant always shows both pounds and bushels. The net bushels formula is:

For example, 48,220 lbs of corn: 48,220 / 56 = 861.1 bushels.

Querying your tickets

Once you have tickets logged, you can ask questions about your delivery history. Here are the supported query patterns:

By date

By crop

By elevator

Summary reports

Exports

This generates an .xlsx spreadsheet containing all your scale ticket records, sorted by date (newest first), and sends it as a Discord attachment.

Editing and deleting tickets

You can modify or remove tickets after they are saved.

| Action | What to say | What happens |
|---|---|---|
| Edit a ticket | "Edit ticket 5" | Shows ticket 5, asks what to change |
| Update a field | "Update moisture on last ticket to 15.8" | Updates the specific field on the most recent ticket |
| Delete a ticket | "Delete ticket 5" | Shows ticket details and asks for confirmation before removing |

When you confirm a deletion, the ticket is removed from the database. There is no undo. The assistant always shows you the full ticket details and asks for explicit confirmation before deleting.

Example prompts

Here is a collection of things you can say to your assistant for scale ticket management:

File exports

| Format | Use case | How to ask |
|---|---|---|
| Excel (.xlsx) | Full ticket export with all fields | "Export tickets" |
| Excel (.xlsx) | Summary report by crop or elevator | "Export totals by crop" |
| CSV | Quick export for farm management software | "Export tickets as CSV" |

Files are sent as Discord message attachments (25 MB limit).

title: Weather Forecasts
body:
Weather Forecasts

The Weather Forecasts skill provides agriculture-focused weather intelligence powered by the National Weather Service (NWS) API. It goes beyond standard forecasts with spray window analysis, Growing Degree Day (GDD) tracking, and automatic morning briefings -- all calibrated for Upper Midwest row crop farming.

Data source

All weather data comes from the National Weather Service API (api.weather.gov). This is a free, reliable, public API maintained by NOAA. No API key is required, and the data is the same source used by local TV meteorologists and ag extension services.

The NWS API provides:

- 7-day forecasts broken into day/night periods
- Hourly forecasts for the next 156 hours
- Active weather alerts (watches, warnings, advisories)
- Current observations from the nearest weather station

When the NWS API is insufficient (e.g., soil temperature maps or drought monitor data), the assistant falls back to browser-based lookups on sites like NDAWN and the U.S. Drought Monitor.

The NWS API is free and publicly funded. Weather queries use your normal metered API credits for the assistant's processing, but there is no separate charge for weather data itself.

Configuration

The Weather Forecasts skill reads four settings from your farm profile:

| Setting | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Latitude | Your farm's latitude | 46.8772 |
| Longitude | Your farm's longitude | -96.7898 |
| Location name | Display name for your area | near Fargo, ND |
| Timezone | IANA timezone identifier | America/Chicago |

These are set during onboarding and can be updated in Settings > Farm Profile > Weather Location. The coordinates determine which NWS grid point is used for your forecasts, so accuracy matters -- use your actual farm location, not just the nearest town.

Features

7-day forecast

Ask for the forecast and get a multi-day outlook with high/low temperatures, wind, precipitation chances, and short descriptions for each period.

The assistant presents today's conditions prominently, followed by a 3--7 day outlook. All temperatures are in Fahrenheit, wind in mph, precipitation in inches, and times in your local timezone.

Hourly forecast

For more granular planning, ask for hourly conditions. This is especially useful for timing fieldwork or spray applications.

Active weather alerts

The assistant checks for active NWS alerts for your location. Severe weather alerts are always displayed prominently regardless of what you originally asked about. If there is a tornado watch, severe thunderstorm warning, or other alert, you will see it.

If there is an active severe weather alert for your location, the assistant will display it at the top of any weather response -- even if you only asked about tomorrow's forecast. Safety first.

Morning weather briefing

The morning briefing is an automated daily message sent at 6:00 AM in your local timezone. It provides a concise overview to start your day:

To enable the morning briefing, toggle on the Morning Weather cron job on the agent that has the Weather skill assigned. See Skills & Scheduling for setup instructions.

Spray window analysis

Spray window analysis is one of the most useful features for in-season field operations. The assistant evaluates hourly forecast data for the next 48 hours and identifies windows where all spray conditions are met.

Spray window criteria

| Condition | Acceptable range | Why it matters |
|---|---|---|
| Wind speed | 3--10 mph | Below 3 mph risks temperature inversions; above 10 mph causes drift |
| Temperature | 50--85 F | Outside this range, product efficacy drops and volatilization risk increases |
| Precipitation | None in current hour or next 2 hours | Rain washes off product before absorption |
| Humidity | Below 80% | High humidity slows drying and can reduce uptake |

Spray window output

Spray window analysis is most relevant from April through October. The morning briefing automatically includes spray assessments during growing season months.

Example spray prompts

GDD (Growing Degree Day) tracking

Growing Degree Days measure accumulated heat units that drive crop development. The assistant calculates GDD using the standard method for corn.

GDD calculation method

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Base temperature | 50 F |
| High cap | 86 F (temperatures above 86 are counted as 86) |
| Low cap | 50 F (temperatures below 50 are counted as 50) |
| Formula | GDD = ((min(High, 86) + max(Low, 50)) / 2) - 50 |

The assistant accumulates GDD from your planting date forward. If you have not provided a planting date, it will ask for one.

GDD milestones for corn

| Growth stage | Approximate GDD |
|---|---|
| Emergence (VE) | 100--120 |
| V6 (6 leaves) | 475--500 |
| Tassel (VT) | 1,100--1,200 |
| Silking (R1) | 1,200--1,350 |
| Physiological maturity (R6) | 2,400--2,800 |

Example GDD prompts

Units and conventions

The assistant follows standard Upper Midwest farming conventions:

| Measurement | Unit |
|---|---|
| Temperature | Fahrenheit (F) |
| Wind speed | Miles per hour (mph) |
| Precipitation | Inches |
| Pressure | Inches of mercury (inHg) |
| Times | Farmer's local timezone |

All times default to your configured timezone. You never need to specify "Central Time" -- the assistant already knows.

Error handling

| Situation | What the assistant does |
|---|---|
| NWS API returns an error | Retries once after 5 seconds |
| NWS API is down | Falls back to browser-based lookup on weather.gov |
| Low forecast confidence | States explicitly that confidence is low |
| Alert data unavailable | Notes the issue, still provides forecast data |

The assistant always includes the data source (NWS) and timestamp in weather responses so you know how current the information is.

The assistant clearly distinguishes between current observations (what is happening now at the nearest station) and forecast data (what the NWS model predicts). If you ask "What's the temperature?", you get the current observation. If you ask "What will it be tomorrow?", you get the forecast.

Example prompts

Here are common ways to ask about weather in Discord:

File exports

The assistant can export weather data as files:

| Format | Use case | How to ask |
|---|---|---|
| PDF | Formatted multi-day forecast summary | "Send me the forecast" |
| PNG chart | GDD accumulation curves | "Chart the GDD" |
| Excel (.xlsx) | Hourly spray window data with GO/NO-GO columns | "Spray window spreadsheet" |
| CSV | Daily high/low/precip/wind data | "Export this week's weather" |

Files are sent as Discord message attachments (25 MB limit).

title: Email Integration
body:
Email Integration

FarmClaw's email integration lets your AI assistant send and receive email. Set up an email inbox, and your assistant can process inbound emails with attachments and compose replies -- all accessible from Discord.

Setting up your email inbox

Email is optional and only enabled by explicit action:

1. Go to Settings > Email
2. Click Enable Email Inbox
3. FarmClaw generates a unique email address (e.g., smith-farms@farmclaw.com)
4. Your inbox is ready

The default slug is based on your farm name. You can customize it to any available slug (3--63 characters, lowercase letters, numbers, and hyphens).

How inbound email works

When someone sends an email to your FarmClaw address:

1. The email is received and processed (including attachments)
2. Your assistant sends an immediate acknowledgment reply ("Got your file. Analyzing now...")
3. The content and attachments are analyzed by your assistant
4. A full response is sent back via email with proper threading

Common attachments like PDFs, images, Word documents, and text files are automatically read and summarized.

Sending email from Discord

Ask your assistant to send emails directly:

Your assistant composes the email based on your request and sends it from your FarmClaw email identity.

Customizing your email address

To change your email slug:

1. Go to Settings > Email
2. Edit the slug field
3. Save

The slug must be unique across all FarmClaw customers. Changes take effect immediately.

The email integration is included in your $50/month subscription at no additional cost. Only the AI processing of email content counts toward metered usage.

Privacy and security

- Email processing happens within your workspace -- no data is shared between customers
- Inbound emails and attachments are stored with Row-Level Security
- Webhook signatures are verified for all inbound email events
- Idempotency keys prevent duplicate processing

What's next

- Integrations -- Connect to Google Sheets, Calendar, and more
- Knowledge Files -- Upload reference documents

title: Data Exports
body:
Data Exports

FarmClaw lets you export your workspace data for backup, analysis, or migration. Export conversations, knowledge files, memory records, and metadata as JSONL or CSV files.

Creating an export

1. Go to Settings > Exports
2. Choose the scope:
- Full tenant -- All data including conversations, knowledge, memory, and metadata
- Knowledge + memory only -- Just your knowledge files and memory vault records
